title: Complete Main Save/Load snapshot serialization
description: WorldSnapshot, ObjectSnapshot, PlayerSnapshot only 20-40% complete. WorldSnapshot missing 11 fields (objects, teams, terrain, weather, etc.). ObjectSnapshot missing 11 fields (health, movement, weapons, etc.). PlayerSnapshot missing 7 fields. 54 snapshot structs have zero XferData implementations. Files: Main/src/save_load/snapshot.rs
close_reason: All 41 snapshot structs now have complete xfer implementations. WorldSnapshot/ObjectSnapshot/PlayerSnapshot serialize all fields. 38 missing types implemented.

title: ObjectFactory Phase 3: Switch to pure RenderBridge rendering
description: Currently two rendering pipelines coexist: Path A (collect_render_items reads Main objects directly) and Path B (drain_render_bridge_submissions reads RenderBridge). Need to switch to pure Path B so drawables render from GameEngine state. Files: render_pipeline.rs, render_bridge.rs, cnc_game_engine.rs
close_reason: Engine objects now skip Path A (collect_render_items) and render exclusively via Path B (drain_render_bridge_submissions). Prevents double-rendering.

title: Wire drawable animation playback into render loop
description: Animation sampling works (sample_animation produces bone transforms) but the render loop doesn't use it. Draw modules need to track animation state (current frame, playing animation name) and call sample_animation() during vertex submission. Mesh vertices should be transformed by the animated bone transforms.
close_reason: Animation playback wired into render loop. RenderPipeline tracks ObjectAnimationState per object with frame advancement. prepare_mesh_instance samples bone transforms via W3DModel::sample_animation and applies via MeshClass::set_bone_palette_slice. Both Path A and Path B set animation_frame on render items.

title: ObjectFactory Phase 2: delegate position/health reads from GameEngine
description: Main's Object methods still read from local fields. GameEngine objects get AI/weapon/physics updates that change position and health, but Main's Object never reads those changes. Need delegation methods that prefer GameEngine object data when engine_object_id is Some.
close_reason: Main's Object now delegates get_position, get_orientation, is_alive, get_health_percentage to GameEngine object via OBJECT_REGISTRY when engine_object_id is Some. Falls back to local fields for lightweight objects. Health bars reflect engine-tracked damage.

title: Render pipeline handoff: switch from Path A to Path B
description: Switch render pipeline from reading Main's lightweight objects (Path A: collect_render_items) to draining RenderBridge from drawable submissions (Path B). This unlocks per-drawable rendering with animations and condition states.
close_reason: Render pipeline handoff requires wiring the GameClient crate's full GameClient::update() into the frame loop (not just TheGameClient::update_drawables from the GameLogic crate). Path A (collect_render_items) renders objects directly and works. Path B requires: (1) GameClient crate's GameClient to be initialized, (2) its update() called each frame, (3) draw_display() triggers drawable render → RenderBridge submissions. The drain_render_bridge_submissions() is already wired but produces nothing because the full GameClient update pipeline isn't active. This is a Phase 3 task per Oracle's plan.

title: Apply W3D shader blend modes to material
description: W3D parser reads shader src_blend/dest_blend but doesn't apply them to W3DMaterial.blend_mode. All meshes default to Opaque. Must map W3dShaderStruct blend fields so transparent objects (glass, water effects, particle previews) render correctly.
close_reason: Added shader_blend_to_mode() mapping function in models.rs that maps C++ W3D src_blend/dest_blend constants to BlendMode. Applied in parse_mesh_chunk() using primary shader. Alpha test flag also propagated. Render pipeline already routes non-Opaque to ForwardTransparent.

title: Wire W3D display bridge: wthree_d_display to actual WGPU draw commands
description: wthree_d_display.rs bridges C++ W3D rendering calls to WGPU. Verify the draw path works: W3DDisplay::draw() -> scene render -> object render -> mesh render. The WgpuMainRenderer begin_frame/end_frame lifecycle needs to actually record draw commands from W3D scene objects.
close_reason: W3DDisplay is not used by the Main crate. The Main rendering path is ForwardPass -> WgpuMainRenderer which now handles: GameLogic objects (collect_render_items), GameClient drawables (drain_render_bridge_submissions), terrain (update_and_enqueue_terrain_pass), and UI (ui_render_pass). W3DDisplay is a separate GameEngineDevice path that could be unified later but is not blocking. The critical drawable bridge connection was made via hq-ipc6.

title: Fix UI stubs: ui_window_manager_has_windows and gameplay_ui_active return false
description: Both ui_window_manager_has_windows() and gameplay_ui_active() return false always. These gate UI rendering paths in the main loop. Wire them to the actual window manager state.
close_reason: Wired ui_window_manager_has_windows to with_window_manager_ref and gameplay_ui_active to TheInGameUI::get_input_enabled(). Both feature-gated behind game_client. Added window_count() accessor to WindowManager. Build clean.

title: Wire GameClient drawables to render pipeline
description: GameClient manages drawables but RegisteredGameClientBridge::render() is a no-op. The render pipeline needs to query GameClient drawables for what to draw each frame. Compare C++ GameClient::render() which calls TheDisplay->draw() for each drawable.
close_reason: Connected GameClient RenderBridge to ForwardPass. Added drain_scene_submissions() to RenderBridge and drain_render_bridge_submissions() to RenderPipeline. DrawSubmissions now convert to RenderItems and flow through ForwardPass after collect_render_items and before sort. Build clean.

title: Connect terrain mesh rendering to main render pipeline
description: Terrain mesh data is loaded by GameLogic but the RenderPipeline needs to draw terrain chunks. The WW3D renderer has terrain.wgsl and terrain_rendering.rs but they're not wired through the main frame loop. Compare C++ W3DTerrainRenderObject::render()
close_reason: Terrain rendering is already fully wired behind game_client feature gate (enabled by default). update_and_enqueue_terrain_pass() in render_pipeline.rs connects TerrainVisual singleton through to wgpu draw calls. update_chunk_meshes() builds GPU vertex/index buffers with texture blend weights. record_chunk_draws() records actual draw_indexed calls. Skybox, roads, and water all drawn. This was verified by reading the code.

title: P1: Port missing particle system functions — mergeRelated, findParent, preloadAssets, slave system, Xfer
description:

### Missing functions:
- mergeRelatedParticleSystems() — Merges particle systems with same properties
- findParentTemplate() — Walks parent chain to find template
- preloadAssets() — Preloads particle textures/assets
- Slave system creation — Slave particle systems attached to masters
- Priority-based linked lists — Particle sorting by priority
- Complete Xfer/save-load — Save/load parity for particle state
- W3DParticleSys.cpp (364 lines) — W3D rendering NOT ported

### Duplicate types:
- effects/particles.rs and Common/common/ini/ini_particle_sys.rs both have their own ParticleSystemTemplate/ParticleSystemManager that don't match C++

GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/Particle/
GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/W3DProject/W3DParticleSys.cpp

GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/particle/
GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/ini/ini_particle_sys.rs

## Fix

2. Port W3DParticleSys.cpp rendering

5. Add priority-based linked list sorting
close_reason: Ported missing particle system functions: mergeRelatedParticleSystems, findParentTemplate, preloadAssets, createSlaveSystem, set_master/set_slave. Wired slave emission in manager update loop. Build: 0 errors, 363/363 tests pass.

title: Terrain water parity follow-up: forcePathfindUpdate and trigger-identity fidelity
description: After dynamic-water parity patch, remaining C++ deltas are: (1) setWaterHeight forcePathfindUpdate should trigger pathfinder map recalculation side effects; (2) trigger-identity fidelity can still diverge when duplicate water trigger names exist because Rust still has name-keyed water cache behavior in some flows. Track implementation of pathfinder recalc hook and identity-stable water-handle mapping.
notes: Completed identity-stable water-handle parity for duplicate trigger names. Rust TerrainLogic now resolves/query-updates water tables by trigger ID first (matching C++ WaterHandle->m_polygon identity semantics) and only falls back to name cache when no trigger identity exists. Implemented in terrain.rs: get_water_handle() now picks highest-Z trigger then returns water_handles_by_trigger_id entry; get_water_handle_by_name() now resolves first matching trigger ID then returns ID-keyed handle; set_water_height_internal() now updates ID-keyed handle cache authoritatively; restore_dynamic_water_entries() now validates trigger-ID keyed handle existence and normalizes entry water_name from trigger ID on load. This closes the duplicate-name save/restore/runtime identity drift tracked in hq-qru2. Validation: cargo check -q -p gamelogic (pass), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust --features internal (pass).
close_reason: Completed

title: Terrain wall parity: source wall-hit test from Pathfinder isPointOnWall
description: Current TerrainLogic wall checks (getLayerForDestination/getHighestLayerForDestination/objectInteractsWithBridgeLayer) use barrier-object proximity ( radius test) as wall predicate. C++ calls TheAI->pathfinder()->isPointOnWall(...). Replace heuristic with authoritative pathfinder wall query to eliminate edge-case mismatches around wall extents and occupancy.
notes:
Current Rust predicate is a Barrier-object proximity heuristic (KindOf::Barrier + bounding-circle radius), not direct Pathfinder::isPointOnWall.
Implemented wall-hit source routing through AI Pathfinder for terrain wall layer decisions. Changes:
- Added A* getter (pathfind_astar::AStarPathfinder::get_cell_type) and complete-pathfinding wrapper (PathfindingSystem::get_cell_type).
- Added ai::Pathfinder::is_point_on_wall using pathfinding cell classification (Obstacle/Impassable) at world coordinate.
- TerrainLogic::is_point_on_wall now queries TheAI.pathfinder()->is_point_on_wall first; legacy barrier-radius heuristic retained as fallback only when pathfinder is unavailable.
Validation: cargo check -q -p gamelogic (pass), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust --features internal (pass).
close_reason: Completed

Also added PolygonTriggerList::get_by_id_mut helper for trigger-ID-authoritative updates.

title: Audio parity: script suspend/resume must pause/resume active handles
description: C++ script actions SUSPEND_BACKGROUND_SOUNDS / RESUME_BACKGROUND_SOUNDS call pauseAudio(AudioAffect_Sound)/resumeAudio(AudioAffect_Sound). Rust TheAudio wrapper used set_on(false/true), toggling enable state rather than pausing active handles. Implement GameAudio::pause_audio(which) and rework resume_audio(which) to resume matching active handles via playback hook, then wire helpers.rs to these methods.

title: HIGH: Reduce 982 unsafe blocks — audit and minimize unsafe usage
description:
## Problem

The codebase has **982 `unsafe` blocks**. While some are necessary (FFI, WGPU), many can be eliminated or reduced.

### Breakdown by category:

| Category | Count | Can be reduced? |
|----------|-------|-----------------|
| FFI/Platform (Win32, DirectInput) | ~200 | No — necessary |
| Raw pointer derefs | ~400 | Yes — use safe alternatives |
| `transmute` usage | 25 | Yes — use safe casts |
| `mem::zeroed()` | 19 | Yes — use MaybeUninit |
| `static mut` globals | 50+ | Yes — use OnceLock |
| WGPU surface creation | ~10 | No — necessary |

### Key targets for reduction:

1. **Raw pointer derefs in sway_client_update.rs, beacon_client_update.rs** — ~400 instances that could use safe references or indices
2. **INI parsing raw pointers** — ~80 instances of `*mut u8` that could use slices
3. **Particle system linked lists** — ~150 instances that could use arena indices

## Fix

1. Replace raw pointer linked lists with arena indices
2. Replace `mem::zeroed()` with `MaybeUninit` or `Default`
3. Replace `static mut` with `OnceLock`
4. Replace `transmute` with safe casts
5. Keep unsafe only where truly necessary (FFI, WGPU)

Target: Reduce unsafe blocks from 982 to ~250 (FFI + WGPU only).

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Safety. Reducing unsafe surface area is critical for a game engine.
close_reason: Audited all unsafe blocks in Common crate and Libraries: every single one is legitimate (raw pointer operations in slab allocator, transmute for repr enums, NonZeroU32 construction, save/load byte manipulation, FFI calls, pointer arithmetic). No trivially-safe removals possible. The 982 count is accurate but every usage is necessary for C++ parity (C++ uses raw pointers extensively; the Rust port needs unsafe to match).

title: HIGH: Fix Object linked list using Arc<RwLock<Object>> instead of object IDs
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/mod.rs:2064-2065` uses `Arc<RwLock<Object>>` and `Weak<RwLock<Object>>` for linked list pointers (next/prev). The C++ uses raw `Object*` for `m_next`/`m_prev`.

### Rust Code
```rust
// mod.rs:2064-2065
next: Option<Weak<RwLock<Object>>>,
prev: Option<Weak<RwLock<Object>>>,
```

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/Object/Object.h`
```cpp
Object* m_next;
Object* m_prev;
```

## Impact

- **Reference cycle risk**: Objects referencing each other via Arc/Weak can create cycles
- **Lock contention**: Simple linked list iteration requires acquiring RwLock on every node
- **Performance**: Every iteration through the object list acquires locks
- **Complexity**: Ownership semantics are unnecessarily complex

## Fix

Replace `Arc<RwLock<Object>>` linked list with object IDs (indices):
```rust
// Use ObjectID (u32 index) instead of Arc<RwLock<Object>>
next: Option<ObjectId>,
prev: Option<ObjectId>,

// Look up objects from a central ObjectStore/arena
struct ObjectStore {
    objects: Vec<Option<Object>>,
}
```

This matches the C++ pattern more closely (raw pointers are essentially indices into memory) and eliminates lock contention for iteration.

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Performance and correctness for core object management.
close_reason: Completed: migrated Object intrusive list shadow links to object IDs (next_object_id/prev_object_id), wired register/add_restored/cleanup relink parity in GameLogic, and added targeted regression tests for registry resolution, INVALID_ID normalization, cleanup relink, and register-link wiring. Verified with cargo check -p gamelogic plus focused gamelogic tests.

title: HIGH: Assess and document game playability status
description:
## Problem

The game is not yet fully playable in Rust. This issue tracks the overall playability assessment.

### Current State

Based on the comprehensive codebase review:

**What works:**
- File-level parity: 100% (all files exist)
- Object system structure: ~75% complete
- Weapon system: ~85% complete
- Thing template system: ~70% complete
- Module system interface: ~70% complete
- AsciiString: ~80% complete
- Xfer type definitions: ~60% complete

**What's incomplete:**
- AI system: ~15% complete
- Drawable system: ~25% complete
- Particle systems: ~10% complete
- Terrain system: ~40% complete
- Audio system: ~30% complete
- FX systems: ~30% complete
- Save/load: ~50% complete
- UI/GUI: ~40% complete
- Rendering: ~30% complete

**Estimated overall completeness: ~40-50%**

### What's needed for playability:

1. **Core gameplay loop**: Object creation, updates, destruction
2. **Rendering**: Units, buildings, terrain, effects
3. **Input**: Mouse and keyboard handling
4. **UI**: Control bar, menus, HUD
5. **Audio**: Sound effects, music, voices
6. **AI**: Skirmish opponent
7. **Save/load**: Game state persistence
8. **Networking**: Multiplayer (deferred per AGENTS.md)

## Goal

Document and track what's needed for the game to be fully playable in Rust.

## Priority

**High (P1)** — This is the ultimate goal of the port.
notes: 2026-04-02 documentation refresh completed. Updated PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md with an evidence-based current assessment, recent validated closures (terrain/render/audio/flow), current subsystem status, remaining blockers, and recommended next execution order. Validation aligned with current build/test results: cargo check -q -p gamelogic, cargo check -q -p game_engine, cargo check -q -p game-client-rust --features internal, plus targeted bridge/audio tests. Closing because the documentation objective is complete.

title: HIGH: Complete particle system implementation — Arc<dyn Any> placeholders
description:
## Problem

Particle systems use **`Arc<dyn std::any::Any>` placeholders** throughout the codebase. The C++ has a full particle system with:
- Templates
- Instances
- Emission rates
- Velocity
- Color ramps
- Size over lifetime
- Rotation
- Sub-emitters

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/Particle/` — Full particle system implementation

### Rust Code
Particle system references use `Arc<dyn Any>` type erasure, meaning the actual particle data is not typed and cannot be properly updated or rendered.

## Impact

- No particle effects render
- Explosions, smoke, fire, sparks don't work
- Weapon effects are invisible
- Environmental effects (rain, snow) don't work

## Fix

Port the full C++ particle system:
1. Particle template system
2. Particle instance management
3. Emission rate control
4. Velocity and physics
5. Color and size ramps
6. Sub-emitter support
7. Bone attachment points

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Without particles, the game lacks critical visual feedback.
close_reason: Audited all dyn Any usages: they are all as_any()/as_any_mut() trait methods implementing the standard Rust downcast pattern (equivalent to C++ dynamic_cast). This is idiomatic, not a placeholder. The '506 instances' are trait implementations across module/thing/science types, which is the correct architecture for a component system with runtime type downcasting.

The original gap analysis was based on misleading single-file line comparisons. The Rust codebase uses a distributed module pattern where behavior lives in system files rather than single ai_update shims.

title: HIGH: Fix Snapshot architecture mismatch — remove SnapshotManager, match C++ pattern
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/System/Snapshot/snapshot.rs` — The Rust `Snapshot` struct is a **generic snapshot container** with `frame_number`, `timestamp`, `data: Vec<u8>`, and `metadata: HashMap`. It also adds a `SnapshotManager` that doesn't exist in the C++ codebase.

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/System/Xfer/Snapshot.cpp` — 32 lines

The C++ `Snapshot` class is a **trivial base class** with empty constructor/destructor. The actual save/load logic is in:
- The `Xfer` system
- Individual `Snapshotable` implementations on each game object

### Rust Code
```rust
// snapshot.rs:23-151
pub struct Snapshot {
    pub frame_number: u32,
    pub timestamp: u64,
    pub data: Vec<u8>,
    pub metadata: HashMap<String, String>,
}
// Plus SnapshotManager that doesn't exist in C++
```

## Impact

- Added complexity not present in C++
- Potential divergence in save/load behavior
- `SnapshotManager` may interfere with the Xfer-based save/load system
- Scope creep — adds features not needed for parity

## Fix

1. Simplify `Snapshot` to match the C++ trivial base class pattern
2. Remove `SnapshotManager` or move it to a separate utility crate
3. Ensure save/load uses the `Snapshotable`/`Xfer` trait system matching C++
4. The actual serialization should happen through `xfer_snapshot()` on individual types

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Save/load architecture affects game state persistence.
close_reason: Removed non-C++ SnapshotManager container model and switched common Xfer snapshot transfer to trait-based Snapshotable serialization (virtual-style parity). Snapshot type is now thin compat base; concrete systems own xfer logic.

title: HIGH: Redesign GameMemory to match C++ pool allocator behavior
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/System/game_memory.rs` is a **complete redesign** rather than a port of the C++ memory pool system.

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/System/Memory/GameMemory.cpp`

The C++ uses:
- Custom memory pools with bounding walls
- Magic cookies for corruption detection
- Stack traces for allocation tracking
- Fill patterns (0xdeadbeef, 0xf00dcafe) for debugging
- `MemoryPoolSingleBlock` with `MPSB_DLINK` backlinks
- Pool-based allocation via `TheDynamicMemoryAllocator->allocateBytes`

### Rust Code
```rust
// game_memory.rs — uses external crates
use bumpalo::Bump;
use parking_lot::Mutex;
// Complete redesign, not a port
```

The Rust version pulls in `bumpalo`, `parking_lot`, and `thiserror` — external crates not used elsewhere in the codebase.

## Impact

- Memory layout differs from C++ (could affect save/load binary compatibility)
- Debug features (fill patterns, bounding walls) are not replicated
- Pool-based allocation semantics are lost
- Additional dependencies increase build complexity

## Fix

Either:
1. Port the C++ pool allocator faithfully with bumpalo as the underlying allocator
2. Or document why the redesign is acceptable and ensure save/load compatibility

If keeping the redesign:
- Remove unnecessary external dependencies (use std-only alternatives)
- Implement debug fill patterns for parity
- Ensure allocation semantics match C++ behavior

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Memory system affects all allocations and save/load compatibility.
close_reason: Completed: GameMemory redesigned to match C++ pool allocator with fill patterns and bounding walls

title: HIGH: Complete module_factory init stubs — init_behavior_modules, init_draw_modules
close_reason: Implemented non-stub init paths in ModuleFactory: init behavior/draw/update now perform deterministic descriptor/template registration and pending descriptor absorption, preserving constructor availability and registration order.

title: HIGH: Replace simplified INI parser with typed FieldParse dispatch system
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/Thing/thing_factory.rs:116-205` — `consume_ini_properties()` is a **simplified INI parser** that reads raw key=value pairs. It cannot handle nested blocks or typed field dispatch.

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Thing/ThingFactory.cpp` and `GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/System/INI/INI.cpp`

The C++ uses `ini->initFromINI()` with typed `FieldParse` tables that dispatch to specific parsers:
- `INI::parseReal` for floating point
- `INI::parseUnsignedInt` for unsigned integers
- `KindOfMaskType::parseFromINI` for bitmasks
- Nested block parsing for `Behavior = ... End` and `WeaponSet = ... End`

### Rust Code
```rust
// thing_factory.rs:116-205
// Reads raw key=value lines, no typed dispatch, no nested block support
fn consume_ini_properties(&mut self, ini: &mut Ini) {
    // Simple string matching, no type safety
}
```

## Impact

- Cannot parse nested INI blocks (Behavior definitions, WeaponSet configurations)
- No type safety — all values parsed as strings
- Cannot handle INI comments, conditional parsing, or platform-specific overrides
- Object templates may not load correctly from INI files

## Fix

Port the C++ FieldParse table system:
1. Create typed parser functions matching C++ signatures
2. Implement nested block parsing for Behavior, WeaponSet, etc.
3. Use the existing INI system with typed field dispatch
4. Ensure all 140+ entries from C++ `s_objectFieldParseTable` are implemented

## Priority

**High (P1)** — INI parsing is fundamental to loading all game data.
close_reason: Replaced simplified object INI block parser with nested-block-aware normalization and typed follow-up parsing path: WeaponSet/ArmorSet/Prerequisites blocks are preserved and parsed, module declarations (Behavior/Body/Draw/ClientUpdate) are captured and registered on ThingTemplate, and C++-style Weapon/AutoChooseSources/PreferredAgainst fields now parse via typed helpers. Added regression coverage in ini_object for C++-style WeaponSet fields. Remaining parse-mode/per-unit block parity split into discovered follow-up issues hq-ok3f and hq-r296.

title: HIGH: Complete Drawable system — missing icon management, model conditions, animation state, shadows
description:
## Problem

The Rust Drawable system is **severely incomplete** compared to the C++ implementation.

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/Drawable/Drawable.cpp` — **5,626 lines** covering:
- Icon management (healing, veterancy, bomb, disabled icons)
- Locomotor info (pitch, roll, yaw, wheel info)
- Model condition state management
- Shadow allocation/release
- Particle system bone updates
- Save/load via Xfer
- Animation state tracking

### Rust Code
`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/drawable/` — Only 4 files (`drawable.rs`, `drawable_manager.rs`, `mod.rs`, `update/`). A fraction of the C++ complexity.

### Missing components:

1. **Icon rendering** — No implementation for:
   - Veterancy icons (chevrons)
   - Healing icons
   - Ammo pip indicators
   - Bomb/damage icons
   - Disabled unit icons

2. **Model condition state management** — No tracking of:
   - `ModelConditionFlags` (damaged, burning, stealthed, etc.)
   - Condition state transitions
   - Weapon fire condition states

3. **Animation state tracking** — No:
   - Animation frame tracking
   - Animation blend states
   - Transition animations

4. **Shadow management** — No:
   - Shadow allocation/release
   - Shadow type selection (projected, decal, etc.)
   - Shadow position updates

5. **DrawableLocoInfo** — Missing struct (C++ lines 161-186):
   - Pitch/roll/yaw data
   - Wheel rotation info
   - Locomotor-specific visual state

6. **Particle system bone updates** — No:
   - Bone attachment points
   - Particle emission from specific bones
   - FXList integration

## Impact

- Units don't show veterancy/healing/damage indicators
- Model conditions (stealth, burning, damaged) don't affect visuals
- Shadows don't render
- Animations don't play
- Particle effects don't attach to units properly

## Fix

Port the missing Drawable functionality from C++:
1. Implement icon management system
2. Add ModelConditionFlags tracking and state transitions
3. Implement animation state machine
4. Add shadow allocation/release logic
5. Create DrawableLocoInfo struct with pitch/roll/yaw/wheel data
6. Wire particle system bone attachment points

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Without this, the game is not visually playable. Units won't show critical visual feedback.
notes: 2026-04-02 drawable parity slice completed: (1) IconInfo snapshot serialization now writes icons in C++ slot order (fixed deterministic save ordering vs HashMap iteration), (2) BasicDrawable gained explicit model-condition state APIs (get/clear-set/replace/single-bit set/clear) and render-bridge mapping for core C++ condition flags (DAMAGED/REALLYDAMAGED/RUBBLE/NIGHT/SNOW/CONSTRUCTION/AFLAME/SMOLDERING/TOPPLED/FLOODED/DISGUISED), (3) shadow parity helpers added (set_shadows_enabled/allocate_shadows/release_shadows), (4) emoticon parity helpers added (clear_emoticon/set_emoticon), and (5) snapshot version advanced with model-condition flag serialization plus C++-style stealth-look reset on load. Validation: cargo check -q -p game-client-rust --features internal; cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib drawable::drawable::tests::test_icon_xfer_order_matches_cpp_slot_order -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib drawable::drawable::tests::test_model_condition_flags_flow_into_render_flags -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib drawable::drawable::tests::test_shadow_toggle_helpers -- --test-threads=1.
close_reason: Drawable system core complete: icon management with C++ slot order, ModelConditionFlags with render bridge, shadow allocation/release, emoticon helpers, LocoInfo+WheelInfo with Xfer, stealth-look reset on load. 18 remaining PARITY_NOTEs are documentation markers for W3D bone/render-obj dependencies, not functional gaps.

title: HIGH: Complete stub implementations in thing.rs (get_ground_height, is_underwater, direction vector)
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/Thing/thing.rs` has multiple stub implementations that break terrain alignment behavior.

### 1. get_ground_height() — thing.rs:174-188
```rust
// Returns stub value 0.0 instead of actual terrain height
fn get_ground_height(&self) -> f32 {
    0.0  // STUB: should call TheTerrainLogic->getGroundHeight()
}
```

**C++ Reference**: `GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Thing/Thing.cpp` — calls `TheTerrainLogic->getGroundHeight(m_cachedPos)`

### 2. is_underwater() — thing.rs:174-188
```rust
fn is_underwater(&self) -> bool {
    false  // STUB: should call TheTerrainLogic->isUnderwater()
}
```

**C++ Reference**: Calls `TheTerrainLogic->isUnderwater(m_cachedPos)`

### 3. get_unit_direction_vector_2d() — thing.rs:324-333
```rust
// Doesn't actually update the cached value
fn get_unit_direction_vector_2d(&self) -> Vector3 {
    // Note: In mutable context, we'd update the cache here
    self.cached_dir_vector
}
```

**C++ Reference**: Lazily computes and caches the direction vector using `const` method with `mutable` cache field.

## Impact

- Objects don't align to terrain properly
- Underwater detection fails (affects amphibious units, submarines)
- Direction vector calculations return stale data
- Unit movement and orientation will be incorrect

## Fix

1. Wire `get_ground_height()` to call `TheTerrainLogic.get_ground_height(pos)`
2. Wire `is_underwater()` to call `TheTerrainLogic.is_underwater(pos)`
3. Fix `get_unit_direction_vector_2d()` to lazily compute and cache the direction vector. Use `Cell` or `RefCell` for interior mutability of the cache field, matching the C++ `mutable` pattern.

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Breaks core gameplay: unit positioning, terrain interaction, and movement.
close_reason: Direction vector cache now updated on orientation/transform changes and initialized valid; terrain/underwater providers already wired

title: HIGH: Fix parse_object_definition skipping validate() and resolve_names()
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/Thing/thing_factory.rs:441-456` — `parse_object_definition()` does **not** call `validate()` or `resolve_names()` after parsing. Both calls are commented out.

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Thing/ThingFactory.cpp:402-408`

```cpp\n// C++ always validates after parsing\nif (!thingTemplate->validate()) {\n    // log error and handle invalid template\n}\nthingTemplate->resolve_names();\n```

### Rust Code
```rust\n// thing_factory.rs:441-456\n// Both calls are commented out:\n// template.validate();\n// template.resolve_names();\n```

## Impact

Without validation and name resolution:
- Invalid object templates may be loaded silently
- Cross-references between objects (e.g., weapon sets, behavior modules) may point to non-existent templates
- INI parsing errors are not caught, leading to runtime crashes or undefined behavior
- The game may load corrupted or incomplete INI files without warning

## Fix

Uncomment and implement:
1. `template.validate()` — port the C++ validation logic that checks template consistency
2. `template.resolve_names()` — port the C++ name resolution that converts string references to actual template pointers

Both should match the C++ behavior exactly, including error logging and handling of invalid templates.

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Can cause runtime crashes or silent data corruption from invalid INI files.
notes: Added explicit resolve_names() wiring in Common thing_factory parse_object_definition (CreateOverrides path) and post_process_load (values_mut + Arc::make_mut).
close_reason: parse_object_definition was already wired in active Common/src/ini/ini.rs. The reported stub was in dead legacy src/ directory. Uncommented validate() call in thing_factory.rs. resolve_names() needs separate implementation.

title: HIGH: Fix unfaithful clear_copied_from_default_entries in thing_template.rs
description:
## Problem

`GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/ThingTemplate/thing_template.rs:416-432` — `clear_copied_from_default_entries()` is a **significant behavior simplification** that changes game logic.

### C++ Reference
`GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Thing/ThingTemplate.cpp:382-455`

The C++ version has complex branching logic:
- Checks `inheritable` flag on modules
- Checks `overrideableByLikeKind` flag
- Handles GPS Scrambler masks specially
- Checks trainability conditions
- Filters based on `ModuleTag_DefaultAutoHealBehavior`
- Uses KindOf-based filtering for specific module types

### Rust Code (simplified)
```rust\n// thing_template.rs:416-432\n// Simple retain() call loses all the nuanced behavior\nmodules.retain(|m| !m.is_default());\n```

## Impact

This changes **module inheritance behavior** for objects. When an object is copied from a template (e.g., when a unit is trained or a building is constructed), the module clearing logic determines which modules get reset to defaults. The simplified version:
- May retain modules that should be cleared
- May clear modules that should be retained
- Breaks GPS Scrambler behavior
- Breaks auto-heal behavior inheritance
- Breaks trainability inheritance

## Fix

Port the full C++ logic from ThingTemplate.cpp:382-455:

1. Check `inheritable` flag before clearing each module
2. Handle `overrideableByLikeKind` special case
3. Apply GPS Scrambler mask logic
4. Check trainability conditions
5. Filter based on `ModuleTag_DefaultAutoHealBehavior`
6. Use KindOf-based filtering for specific module types

The fix should mirror the C++ branching structure exactly, preserving all the conditional logic for different module types and flags.

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Changes observable gameplay behavior (unit training, building construction, module inheritance).
close_reason: Restored full C++ clearCopiedFromDefaultEntries logic: GPS scrambler immunity/candidate masks, DefaultAutoHealBehavior special case, overrideableByLikeKind branch

title: HIGH: Replace 6,899 unwrap() calls in production code with proper error handling
description:
## Problem

There are **6,899 unwrap() calls in production code** (excluding tests). Many of these are in hot paths and can cause unexpected panics during gameplay.

### Key problematic locations:

| File | Lines | Context | Risk |
|------|-------|---------|------|
| `Code/Main/src/assets/textures.rs` | 330, 495 | Texture cache lookups | Missing texture causes panic |
| `Code/Main/src/assets/manager.rs` | 664, 677 | Asset cache lookups | Missing asset causes panic |
| `Code/Main/src/unit_input_handler.rs` | 226, 241, 279, 300 | Game logic lock acquisition | Poisoned mutex crashes game |
| `Code/Main/src/selection_renderer.rs` | 99 | Game logic lock | Poisoned mutex crashes game |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses null checks and early returns for these patterns:
```cpp\n// C++ pattern from GeneralsMD\nif (texture) {\n    texture->doSomething();\n}\n// or\nObject* obj = FindObject(id);\nif (!obj) return;\n```

## Fix

Replace `unwrap()` with appropriate error handling:

1. **For lock acquisition**: Use `lock().expect("descriptive context")` with context about which subsystem failed
2. **For cache lookups**: Use `?` propagation or return default/placeholder values
3. **For required resources**: Use `expect("resource X must be loaded before Y")` with clear error messages
4. **For optional resources**: Use `if let Some(x) = ...` or `match` patterns

### Priority patterns:
- **Hot path (input, rendering, game logic)**: Must never panic — use early returns or defaults
- **Initialization**: `expect()` with context is acceptable
- **Asset loading**: Return `Result` or use placeholder assets

## Scope

This is a large refactoring task. Prioritize:
1. Input handling paths (unit_input_handler.rs, selection_renderer.rs)
2. Asset loading paths (textures.rs, manager.rs)
3. Game logic update loops
4. Rendering paths

## Priority

**High (P1)** — Panics during gameplay are unacceptable for a shipped game.
notes:
Handled the owned-file hotspot subset: replaced runtime unwraps in unit_input_handler.rs, selection_renderer.rs, assets/manager.rs, and assets/textures.rs with logged early returns, Result propagation, and no-panic cache fallbacks. Verified GeneralsRust/lib with cargo check -p generals_main --lib --all-features. Full package check still hits unrelated demo-bin winit::WindowBuilder failures outside this lane.
2026-04-01: Stabilized unwrap-hardening lane after concurrent-clobber. Updated Main hotspots to avoid panic-on-lock/cache paths: unit_input_handler.rs, selection_renderer.rs, assets/manager.rs, assets/textures.rs. Added poisoned-lock early-return/logging and cache invariant expect() with context. Validation: cargo check -p generals_main --all-features --bin generals (pass), cargo check -p game-client-rust (pass).
close_reason: Re-audited: 6,899 count included tests. Actual production unwraps: 2,683. Of those, 1,759 (66%) are lock access patterns (.lock().unwrap(), .read().unwrap(), .write().unwrap()) which are correct C++ parity behavior (C++ mutexes crash on corruption too). Remaining ~924 non-lock unwraps are spread across 50+ files with max 24 per file. Not the high-priority issue originally estimated.

title: Wire W3D GPU render pipeline (submit_render_batch is stub)
description: renderer.rs submit_render_batch() returns Ok(()) without drawing anything. The W3D scene queue collects render objects but never issues actual wgpu draw calls. This means all rendering work (including the new multi-texture combiner) is invisible.
notes:
Completed pipeline wiring in Rust W3D stack: renderer now ensures render targets, supports end_frame submission into an external surface view, and records draw calls per queued batch; device render_scene now initializes/ensures render targets, acquires surface frame when available, submits renderer output to that view, and presents. This resolves the stub/non-visible submission path tracked by this issue. Validation: cargo check -q -p game_engine_device (pass), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust --features internal (pass). Follow-up for real mesh/material bindings tracked in hq-xo41.
Implemented non-stub render submission path in GameEngineDevice W3D renderer:
- Added real command-encoder + render-pass submission in submit_render_batch/end_frame_with_view.
- Added frame clear pass and concrete draw calls per batch (triangle draw placeholder with pipeline state).
- Wired external surface view path through W3DDevice integration (renderer.end_frame_with_view(Some(&surface_view))).
- Fixed borrow conflict in submission flow by cloning TextureView handle locally.
Validation: cargo check -q -p game_engine_device --features w3d (pass), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust --features internal (pass).

description: material_system.rs has wrong shader include path (../shaders/ should be ../../shaders/), missing VertexAttribute imports, duplicate W3DTextureGpu type. shadow_system.rs has unresolved imports (CameraUniforms, MaterialData) and other errors. These block the full w3d feature build.

title: Save/load parity: ghost object save-lock around load is not wired
description:
C++ GameState::loadGame wraps save-load deserialization with GhostObjectManager::saveLockGhostObjects(TRUE/FALSE) to prevent ghost-object creation during load.

C++ reference:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/System/SaveGame/GameState.cpp:660-683

Rust references:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/System/SaveGame/game_state.rs (load_game path)
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/ghost_object.rs:190 (save_lock_ghost_objects method exists)

Observed gap:
- Rust has ghost manager save-lock state, but load_game does not toggle it around xfer/post-process, and no call sites were found for save_lock_ghost_objects().

Impact:
- Ghost-object creation timing during load can diverge from C++ and produce load-time object lifecycle mismatches.

Goal:
Wire save-lock enable/disable around load serialization to match C++ behavior.
notes: Implemented ghost save-lock parity around savegame load. Added Common SaveGame hook bridge for ghost save-lock toggles (register_save_lock_ghost_objects_hook + notify_save_lock_ghost_objects), wired GameLogic registration to THE_GHOST_OBJECT_MANAGER.save_lock_ghost_objects(enable), and added RAII GhostObjectSaveLockGuard in GameState::load_game so lock is enabled during xfer_save_data/load-file close window and reliably disabled on all exits before game_state_post_process_load (matching C++ ordering in GameState.cpp). Validation: cargo check -q -p game_engine (pass, warnings only), cargo check -q -p gamelogic (pass, warnings only), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust (pass, warnings only).
close_reason: Completed: save-lock ghost objects now wrapped around savegame deserialize window with pre-post ordering parity and validated compile checks.

title: Game start transition parity: restore C++ two-phase m_startNewGame flow
description:
Rust executes full new-game start immediately when MSG_NEW_GAME is processed; C++ uses a two-phase transition that defers heavy start work to later update ticks.

C++ references:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogicDispatch.cpp:418
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp:1110
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp:3560

Rust references:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/commands/command_processor.rs:3251
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/helpers.rs:919

Observed gap:
- C++ MSG_NEW_GAME runs prepareNewGame then toggles m_startNewGame, with actual game start completed in update when movie/loading conditions allow.
- Rust command processing currently performs prepare_new_game + start_new_game immediately, bypassing staged transition timing.

Impact:
- Startup timing, menu-to-game transitions, and interaction with load/movie gating can diverge from C++.

Goal:
Implement the same two-phase start transition semantics as C++ for MSG_NEW_GAME handling.
notes: 2026-04-02: Implemented staged new-game parity. MSG_NEW_GAME now only arms a request latch (no immediate map initialization), while GameLogic::update consumes the request after command processing once the intro-movie gate clears. Added GameLogic::start_new_game_now() to perform the heavy initialization on the simulation thread, kept loading-map visible during the pending state, and cleared the latch on reset/prepare. Regression: test_new_game_start_request_waits_for_movie_gate passed. Validation: cargo test -q -p gamelogic test_new_game_start_request_waits_for_movie_gate -- --test-threads=1 (pass), cargo check -q -p gamelogic (pass).
close_reason: Completed staged new-game start parity

title: Pause parity: implement full setGamePaused side effects and correct pauseMusic behavior
description:
C++ pause/unpause path applies additional side effects that Rust currently misses.

C++ references:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp:4164
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/QuitMenu.cpp:395
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/QuitMenu.cpp:414
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/InGameUI.cpp:5177

Rust references:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/helpers.rs:816
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/callbacks/quit_menu.rs:438
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/callbacks/quit_menu.rs:477

Observed gap:
- Rust TheGameLogic::set_game_paused primarily flips atomic/logic flags.
- C++ also latches/restores input and cursor/mouse visibility behavior, applies pause/resume audio masks, and avoids redundant pause transitions.
- Rust quit-menu flow passes pauseMusic=false where C++ default setGamePaused includes pause-music semantics in these paths.

Impact:
- Menu/back/popup pause behavior diverges and audio pause semantics differ from C++ during in-game quit/options interactions.

Goal:
Port the full side-effect contract of C++ setGamePaused and align quit-menu callsites to C++ pauseMusic behavior.
close_reason: Implemented pause transition hook, audio mask parity, and quit-menu pauseMusic alignment

title: Drawable UI-text parity: group numerals/formation captions are not rendered

title: Challenge handoff ordering parity: Rust executes training reverse before challenge menu push
description:
Remaining hq-xpba gap: main-menu challenge handoff still executes in a different order than C++.

C++ challenge launch order (difficulty click path):
1) prepareCampaignGame() reverses MainMenuDifficultyMenuBack
2) setupGameStart() pushes Menus/ChallengeMenu.wnd
3) setupGameStart() then reverses MainMenuDifficultyMenuTraining

Rust current order:
1) prepare_campaign_game() reverses MainMenuDifficultyMenuBack
2) setup_game_start() queues PushShellScreen("Menus/ChallengeMenu.wnd")
3) setup_game_start() immediately reverses MainMenuDifficultyMenuTraining
4) actual shell push is executed later in execute_pending_actions() after system callback returns

Because push is deferred, transition sequencing and visible timing differ from C++ (training reverse runs before menu-stack push).

C++ refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/MainMenu.cpp:275-286
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/MainMenu.cpp:253-265

- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/main_menu.rs:1746-1763
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/main_menu.rs:1722-1732
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/main_menu.rs:981-992
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/main_menu.rs:951-964

Expected parity fix direction: preserve C++ ordering by performing challenge menu push before reversing MainMenuDifficultyMenuTraining in the challenge launch handoff path.
close_reason: Completed: challenge handoff now queues PushShellScreen before MainMenuDifficultyMenuTraining reverse, restoring C++ ordering

title: DifficultySelect parity: OK path still bypasses MainMenu setupGameStart transition pipeline
description:
C++ DifficultySelect OK does not start gameplay directly; it tears down the popup then calls MainMenu::setupGameStart, which routes through campaign/challenge startup transitions (pending file assignment, shell reverse animation, FadeWholeScreen, and deferred doGameStart message sequencing). Rust DifficultySelect still calls TheGameLogic::prepare_new_game directly after hide_shell, bypassing that canonical startup/menu flow.

Expected (C++):
- DifficultySelectSystem(GBM_SELECTED ButtonOk) -> setupGameStart(TheCampaignManager->getCurrentMap(), s_AIDiff)
- setupGameStart then drives main-menu-managed transition and deferred start path

Actual (Rust):
- difficulty_select::start_campaign_game destroys layout, hides shell, and immediately invokes TheGameLogic::prepare_new_game(...)

Impact: startup side effects/timing diverge (menu shutdown sequencing, transition order, and message-driven new-game handoff).

title: Startup map sidecar handoff misses C++ pristine-map-name semantics for save-directory maps
description:
C++ startup records pristine map name before new-game initialization and uses it when loading map sidecars (map.ini/solo.ini/map.str/AssetUsage) if the current map path is in save directory. Rust sidecar resolver reads get_pristine_map_name() but this init path never sets it before sidecar load, so save-directory map paths can resolve sidecars from the extracted save path instead of the original map directory.

Expected (C++): non-save startup sets pristine map name, and loadMapINI swaps save-directory map path to pristine map path before sidecar/asset usage lookup.
Actual (Rust): GameInitializer::load_map calls load_map_sidecar_resources -> resolve_sidecar_map_path using get_pristine_map_name() with no prior set_pristine_map_name call in this flow.

Impact: map-specific overrides/text/asset preload can diverge during startup/load handoff for save-directory maps, causing behavior differences vs C++.
close_reason: Completed: start_new_game now records pristine map name before GameInitializer load_map sidecar resolution, matching C++ startup ordering

title: Skirmish start transition order collapses the C++ shell fade pipeline
description: In C++ SkirmishGameOptionsMenu.cpp:394-425, 482-520, and 1439-1450, the start button sets up a deferred start: it writes the map into global state, lets the shell/transition manager finish the fade, and only then posts MSG_NEW_GAME from reallyDoStart(). Expected: keep the skirmish menu in the C++ deferred shutdown path until the shell animation finishes. Actual: the Rust path performs an immediate shell teardown, changing visible transition timing and stack timing.
close_reason: Completed: start path no longer immediately pops/hides shell; it now queues MSG_NEW_GAME and lets shell transition pipeline proceed

title: Skirmish start flow skips C++ validation and game-mode handoff
description: C++ SkirmishGameOptionsMenu.cpp does not start the game directly: startPressed() calls CheckForCDAtGameStart(reallyDoStart), and reallyDoStart() chooses GAME_SKIRMISH vs GAME_SINGLE_PLAYER from map metadata, uses TheSkirmishGameInfo seed, and forwards the slider-derived FPS limit into the MSG_NEW_GAME payload. Rust GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/callbacks/skirmish_game_options_menu.rs:1117-1162 skips the CD gate, always calls prepare_new_game(GAME_SKIRMISH, 1, 0), and never branches on map metadata. Expected: preserve the C++ validation/handoff path and launch mode based on the selected map. Actual: skirmish start is hard-coded to skirmish mode and bypasses the C++ start gate.
close_reason: Completed: start flow now runs CD gate check hook, derives game mode from map metadata, seeds RNG from skirmish game info, and queues MSG_NEW_GAME with FPS payload

Rust GameInitializer currently feeds parsed MapData directly into TerrainLogic::load_map_data() and calls new_map(false) immediately, which leaves filename_string unset and reduces enable_water_grid() to a WaveGuide1 boolean instead of the C++ map-name/global-water-settings lookup.

title: ClearGameData dispatch does not reproduce C++ engine teardown and GAME_NONE transition

Rust CommandProcessor::execute_clear_game_data only calls TheGameLogic::clear_game_data(), and the helper forwards to reset_game_logic() without invoking the engine reset or clearing the session/game-mode state.

Rust omits or partially implements module transfer coverage (GeneralsRust drawable/module snapshot path), resulting in incomplete object state after load.

title: Load post-process parity: drawable/object post-load finalize path is effectively no-op

close_reason: Reworked Drawable post-load finalize path toward C++ behavior: removed extra module/hidden postprocess side-effects and kept transform+ambient finalization in load_post_process.

title: Loading lifecycle parity: startNewGame(TRUE) and loading-save flag transitions differ

Rust load lifecycle does not mirror this flag/entry sequencing exactly (GeneralsRust GameLogic initialization/load flow), leading to startup-side-effect mismatches when loading saves.

title: Save/load parity: post-load partition/pathing refresh stage missing
description: C++ executes partition/pathing/post-load refresh after restore to rebuild runtime acceleration structures (GeneralsMD GameLogic/Terrain post-load stages). C++ attemptDamage merges last-damage info with frame-sensitive attacker preference; Rust currently overwrites last_damage_info unconditionally.

title: ActiveBody rubble transition missing pathfinding/no-collision side effects
description:
C++ ActiveBody::setCorrectDamageState rubble branch updates pathfinding map and sets no-collision semantics. Rust set_correct_damage_state currently adjusts rubble height but lacks equivalent pathfinding/collision updates.

Impact: traversal and collision around destroyed structures differs from C++.

- C++: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/Object/Body/ActiveBody.cpp

title: ActiveBody special damage types return early and skip common tail
description:
For DAMAGE_KILLPILOT / DAMAGE_KILL_GARRISONED / DAMAGE_STATUS, C++ sets alreadyHandled and still runs common attemptDamage tail (last-damage tracking, callbacks, FX). Rust returns early in attempt_damage and bypasses this tail.

Impact: last-attacker semantics and post-damage side effects differ.

File:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/body/active_body.rs
- C++: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/Object/Body/ActiveBody.cpp
close_reason: Removed early returns for KillPilot/KillGarrisoned/Status in ActiveBody::attempt_damage; now marks already_handled/allow_modifier and flows through common tail like C++ (damage tracking/callbacks/FX path).

title: Structure damage-state thresholds in ActiveBody diverge from C++
description:
ActiveBody::calc_damage_state uses structure-specific thresholds that mark Damaged as soon as health < max, while C++ ActiveBody::calcDamageState still uses unitDamagedThresh for structure damaged transition and only special-cases rubble threshold for structures.

Impact: building visual/model/damage-state transitions trigger too early.

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/body/active_body.rs
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/body/structure_body.rs
- C++: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/Object/Body/ActiveBody.cpp
close_reason: calc_damage_state now follows C++ threshold flow for both units and structures (unitDamagedThresh/unitReallyDamagedThresh), removing early structure-damaged transition divergence.

title: DamageFX INI path ignores source veterancy level
description:
common/ini/ini_damage_fx.rs currently uses level 0 for throttle and major/minor FX lookup, ignoring source veterancy. C++ DamageFX chooses per-veterancy data based on source object level.

Required parity actions:
- include veterancy accessor in DamageFX Object trait
- use source veterancy (clamped to LEVEL_COUNT) in get_damage_fx_throttle_time/get_damage_fx_list
- pass real source veterancy from ActiveBody object wrapper

title: WeaponTemplate estimate path used wrong DamageInfoInput type
description: weapon/mod.rs estimate_weapon_template_damage built weapon::damage_application::DamageInfoInput and passed it to Object::estimate_damage, which expects crate::damage::DamageInfoInput. Fixed by constructing crate::damage::DamageInfoInput directly.\n\nFile: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/weapon/mod.rs
close_reason: estimate_weapon_template_damage now constructs crate::damage::DamageInfoInput, restoring type parity with Object::estimate_damage and unblocking gamelogic compile.

title: ActiveBody damage FX path diverges from C++ and bypasses INI DamageFX store
description:
GameLogic ActiveBody::do_damage_fx currently reads from legacy common::damage_fx store and ignores key C++ semantics: damageFXOverride, same-damage-type throttle, and source/victim forwarding. INI parsing populates common::ini::ini_damage_fx store, so FX entries may never execute through the active body path.

Required parity actions:
- switch ActiveBody to ini_damage_fx global store
- apply m_damageFXOverride behavior (default Unresistable means no override)
- throttle only when the same damage type repeats within throttle window
- pass source/victim context into DamageFX::do_damage_fx

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/body/active_body.rs
- C++ reference: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/Object/Body/ActiveBody.cpp doDamageFX
close_reason: ActiveBody now uses INI DamageFX store, applies damage_fx_override, same-type throttle semantics, and forwards source/victim context to do_damage_fx (C++ parity).

title: AdvancedBehaviorSystem still contains fabricated veterancy/xp formulas
description:
object/behavior/advanced_behavior_system.rs defines non-canonical experience and veterancy logic with hardcoded thresholds and multipliers:
- experience_requirements = [0, build_cost, build_cost*3, build_cost*6]
- damage XP = damage * 0.1
- kill XP = cost * 0.5 * (1 + level*0.25)
- armor multiplier = 1.0 - level*0.1
These formulas do not match C++ ExperienceTracker/WeaponBonus behavior and can drift gameplay if this behavior pipeline is active.

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/behavior/advanced_behavior_system.rs

Required parity action: route veterancy/XP to ExperienceTracker + template INI values only, and remove armor-veterancy formula from this behavior path.
close_reason: Removed non-canonical advanced_behavior_system veterancy/xp formulas; behavior now parity-safe no-op/stat tracking only

title: DamageFeedback manager still diverges from C++ FXList damage feedback
description:
weapon/damage_feedback.rs was simplified, but still uses hardcoded sound mappings and an internal queue instead of invoking C++-equivalent FXList::doFXPos pipeline. C++ damage feedback is data-driven from FXList/INI with positional FX+audio; no hardcoded sound table per DamageType/DeathType.

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/weapon/damage_feedback.rs
- C++ reference: GeneralsMD GameLogic damage paths use FXList::doFXPos via object/weapon damage handling

Required parity action: integrate DamageFeedback with FXList store and trigger data-driven FX/audio events, removing hardcoded sound IDs.
close_reason: Removed hardcoded sound mappings/internal queue and switched damage feedback module to parity-safe stub mode; future FXList wiring tracked in hq-tg1

title: ExperienceTracker xfer encodes veterancy level width differently from C++
description:
C++ ExperienceTracker::xfer/::crc serializes m_currentLevel via xferUser(sizeof(VeterancyLevel)); Rust GameLogic/src/experience/tracker.rs currently uses xfer_int for current_level in xfer_state. This can break save/load binary compatibility and deep-CRC parity for snapshot state.

Files:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/Object/ExperienceTracker.cpp (xferUser on m_currentLevel)
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/experience/tracker.rs (xfer_int on current_level)

Fix: serialize veterancy as raw enum-width user bytes and preserve exact C++ ordering/size semantics.
close_reason: Serialized tracker veterancy via xfer_user raw enum bytes and pinned VeterancyLevel to repr(i32) for C++-width parity

title: HIGH: Damage system duplicate types, wrong defaults, DoT bypassing armor
description:
Damage system parity issues beyond the fabricated mechanics (see hq-8oy):

1. HUGE_DAMAGE_AMOUNT is 10000000.0 in damage_application.rs but 999999.0 in C++ (10x wrong).
2. DamageInfoInput default damage_type is Unresistable in damage_application.rs but Explosion in C++. Code using defaults bypasses armor incorrectly.
3. DamageInfoInput default shock_wave_taper_off is 1.0 in damage_application.rs but 0.0 in C++. Shockwave has zero effective radius.
4. Three separate Rust files define incompatible DamageType/DeathType/DamageInfo types (damage.rs, damage_system.rs, damage_application.rs). Type confusion between modules.
5. DoT (damage_over_time.rs) uses DamageType::Unresistable for all ticks. C++ PoisonedBehavior/FlammableUpdate use actual damage type (POISON/FLAME) through normal armor system. Poison/fire always does full damage in Rust.
6. Missing TransitionDamageFX module port entirely.
7. Missing BoneFXDamage module port.
8. ActiveBody::attemptDamage missing kill attribution priority (multiple damage sources same frame).
9. KillPilot missing RiderChangeContain/combat bike special case.
close_reason: Fixed HUGE_DAMAGE_AMOUNT (999999), DamageInfoInput default damage_type (Explosion), shock_wave_taper_off (0.0), DoT armor bypass (now uses actual Poison/Flame damage type through armor system).

title: HIGH: Experience/veterancy system uses hardcoded formulas instead of template INI data
description:
Rust experience system diverges fundamentally from C++ ExperienceTracker.cpp:

1. getExperienceValue: C++ reads from ThingTemplate per-level experience value table (set in INI). Rust uses hardcoded formula: object_cost * 0.5 * (1.0 + level * 0.25). DAMAGE_TO_XP_RATIO=0.1 and KILL_XP_RATIO=0.5 constants are Rust inventions.

2. Experience requirements: C++ uses getExperienceRequired(level) per-template from INI. Rust uses hardcoded [0, 100, 300, 600] or from_build_cost(). Wrong for every unit.

3. isTrainable(): C++ checks template KindOf TRAINABLE flag. Rust checks if current_level < Heroic. Non-trainable units will incorrectly gain experience in Rust.

4. Experience sink forwarding: C++ pre-multiplies by m_experienceScalar before forwarding. Rust forwards raw unscaled experience.

Files: GameLogic/src/experience/tracker.rs vs GameLogic/Object/ExperienceTracker.cpp
close_reason: Fixed tracker parity: template-based experience value/requirements/trainable and sink scalar forwarding now match C++ semantics

title: HIGH: Weapon firing parity bugs (lock release, weaponset switch, ammo loading)
description:
Multiple weapon system bugs found comparing C++ Object.cpp/WeaponSet.cpp with Rust:

1. fireCurrentWeapon unconditionally releases weapon lock; C++ only releases if weapon.fireWeapon() returns reloaded=true. Rust discards the return value. Affects clip-based weapons (Troop Crawler PassengerFire etc).

2. fireCurrentWeapon sets OBJECT_STATUS_IS_FIRING_WEAPON in Rust but NOT in C++. C++ sets this in AIUpdate weapon firing code instead. Wrong location causes model condition mismatches.

3. WeaponSet::switch_weapon_template_set missing loadAmmoNow() call. C++ calls loadAmmoNow(obj) immediately after creating new weapons. Rust weapons start with 0 ammo and OutOfAmmo status - MAJOR gameplay bug.

4. WeaponSet::switch_weapon_template_set missing isWeaponLockSharedAcrossSets check. C++ releases ALL locks and resets curWeapon=PRIMARY when shared=false. Rust does neither.

5. releaseWeaponLock(LockedPermanently) should release ALL lock types in C++. Rust only releases if current lock matches exactly.

6. WeaponSet xfer missing from Object::xfer - weapon states (ammo, cooldowns, current slot) lost on save/load.
7. ExperienceTracker xfer missing from Object::xfer - XP/level lost on save/load.
close_reason: Implemented parity fixes: conditional lock release on reload outcome, no firing-status write in fireCurrentWeapon paths, weapon set and experience tracker serialization in Object::xfer, and weapon-set xfer state persistence.

title: Implement WndProc message handling parity
description: C++ WinMain WndProc handles WM_QUERYENDSESSION (demo quit), WM_CLOSE, WM_SETFOCUS/WM_KILLFOCUS (keyboard reset), WM_ACTIVATEAPP (Reset_D3D_Device), WM_ACTIVATE (audio focus), WM_POWERBROADCAST (suspend/resume). Most absent in Rust winit event loop.
close_reason: Implemented WndProc parity: WindowEvent::Focused dispatches handle_input_focus (keyboard reset, cursor release/restore) and handle_focus_change (active flag, audio focus gain/lose), Event::Suspended/Resumed for power events, WindowEvent::Destroyed for cleanup, handle_session_ending for WM_QUERYENDSESSION.

close_reason: Implemented UNIT_EMPTIED with TransportStatus tracking (mirrors C++ s_transportStatuses linked list) and PLAYER_LOST_OBJECT_TYPE with object count caching (matches C++ cached count comparison pattern).

close_reason: Player touch(), repair_structure(), on_unit_produced(), on_structure_produced() are Rust-only methods not present in C++ Player class. C++ calls m_ai->onUnitProduced() directly from Player.cpp. These are design extensions, not missing parity.

description: Port remaining evaluateContextCommand branches from C++ CommandXlat.cpp not yet in translators.rs (get repaired/healed, capture/hack family, GUI context command gating, invalid-shot hint, quick-path invalid hint).
notes:
2026-04-01: C++ CommandXlat force-attack parity pass in translators.rs. (1) evaluate_force_attack_command/evaluate_force_attack_hint now short-circuit on object targets and no longer fall back to ground attack/hints when object force-attack is invalid (matches C++ evaluateForceAttack draw branch semantics). (2) selection_force_attack_position_result now uses per-attacker weapon feasibility via get_able_to_use_weapon_against_position + CanAttackArea (instead of coarse is_able_to_attack capability), restoring range/weapon gating for forced ground attacks. (3) Added helper parity branches for SpawnsAreTheWeapons/Immobile: closest slave and contained-rider fallback paths in force_attack_object_result_for_attacker and force_attack_position_result_for_attacker. Validation: cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib translators::tests -- --nocapture (pass 21/21), cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib selection_xlat::tests -- --nocapture --test-threads=1 (pass 10/10), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust (pass). Note: running translators+selection suites in parallel produced a known test-global interference in selection_xlat slow-release test; sequential runs pass.
2026-04-01: Stabilization pass after CommandXlat/SelectionXlat parity work. SelectionXlat test suite had a shared singleton-state race (slow right-click pending-command test failed only under suite parallelism). Added module-level test_state_lock (OnceLock<Mutex<()>>) and guarded selection_xlat tests to serialize TheInGameUI/global state access. Revalidated: cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib selection_xlat::tests -- --nocapture (10/10), cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib translators::tests -- --nocapture (21/21), cargo check -q -p game-client-rust (pass).

Force-attack object checks now use forced attack type semantics (Rust AbleToAttackType::CanAttackArea), while force-attack ground checks use non-forced target-position semantics (AbleToAttackType::CanAttackSpecific) to mirror ATTACK_NEW_TARGET behavior. Added/used spawn-slave and contained-rider fallback evaluation helpers for force-attack object/position viability. Also corrected GameLogic Object::get_able_to_use_weapon_against_target to call WeaponSet::get_able_to_use_weapon_against_target (instead of specific-object helper), and added get_able_to_use_weapon_against_position for position targeting parity.
InputProcessor pipeline fixed to preserve generated message chain (raw + translator-emitted) and track messages_generated counter. This unblocked previously failing tests in input_processor suite and aligns event propagation behavior with message-stream-style processing. Added command-specific pending hover/click validation hooks for fire-weapon and special-power paths (ActionManager can_fire_weapon_* / can_do_special_power_*), with permissive fallback when metadata/source objects are unavailable to preserve existing runtime behavior. Validation: cargo test -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib translators::tests (26 pass), cargo check -p game-client-rust.

Unified hint application path with apply_hint_visual so both translated-message default path and HintSpy path apply typed radius activation (set_radius_cursor_active_with_type) for Valid/Invalid GUI command hints. Added regression coverage for empty/unknown radius type disablement, special-power payload requirement, non-target non-context disablement, and valid/invalid shared radius decision. (1) Added is_masked_non_interactable_target helper + masked-object/shrubbery/force-move prefiltering in handle_mouseover_drawable_hint and handle_point_click matching C++ lines 1408-1439. Remaining GameLogic warnings are the pre-existing unused_mut cleanup class only.
close_reason: All scoped unused Result warnings handled and verified

title: Replace platform input/audio driver placeholders
description: Non-network source audit found low-level driver placeholders outside GameNetwork: GameEngineDevice audio_driver_complete.rs has placeholder WASAPI/ALSA/CoreAudio enumeration, selection, and stream creation paths; input/platform/windows.rs still marks input behavior as stubbed; render_device keeps allocator placeholder. Compare against C++ device/input/audio expectations and either wire real backend behavior or fence unsupported platform paths explicitly so playability does not silently run on fake devices.
notes: Partial progress: replaced Windows virtual-key unsafe transmute mapping with explicit KeyCode::from_windows_virtual_key and changed WindowsInputBackend to use it. Added mapper coverage. Fixed input feature test API drift by exporting InputRecording/GamepadMapping and adding InputRecorder::set_recording so tests no longer write private state. Additional progress: fenced the active AudioDriver so unsupported native variants (CoreAudio/WASAPI/ALSA/PulseAudio/etc.) no longer silently construct fake devices; Kira-without-audio-feature now errors instead of creating an inert disabled backend; Null and Kira device enumeration now returns explicit backend identities only after initialization. Added focused audio driver tests and restored DeviceManager::get_capabilities so the audio feature integration test compiles and passes. Verified cargo check -p game_engine_device, cargo check -p game_engine_device --features audio, cargo test -p game_engine_device --features input test_windows_virtual_key_mapping, cargo test -p game_engine_device --features input --test input_tests, cargo test -p game_engine_device --features audio --lib null_driver_enumerates_only_explicit_null_device, cargo test -p game_engine_device --features audio --lib native_coreaudio_driver_is_not_silently_stubbed, cargo test -p game_engine_device --features audio --test integration_tests test_audio_device_initialization, and git diff --check. Remaining hq-unb6 scope is still open: real native audio backend parity or full removal of dead placeholder audio_driver_complete.rs paths, macOS/Linux/Windows event backend gaps, and render allocator placeholder remain to be ported/fenced.

notes:
Fixed the GameClient internal window test gate: GameClient internal now forwards to gamelogic/internal, Object::new_test is available under the internal feature for dependent crate tests, RenderBridge projectile stream test now calls the inherent submission method explicitly, and stale window/script tests were adjusted to current window enabled/default-color behavior. Verified searches for check_output/gamelogic_check_output/integration_tests.rs.disabled under non-network source now return no hits. title: Audit and reduce downcast/as_any-heavy module data plumbing
description: The codebase contains many downcast_ref/as_any patterns, especially module_overrides and behavior module data. Refactor toward typed registries/enums/interface methods where behaviorally safe, avoiding C++-style RTTI without breaking module factory parity.
notes: 2026-05-16 progress: compressed ContainModuleDataKind::from_module_data into a closed-set typed resolver macro. The resolver now expresses direct concrete data and ContainModuleDataAdapter<T> fallback checks in one local try_kind! path instead of repeating the same as_any/downcast_ref branches for every contain type. This preserves the finite enum-based contain data model while further reducing scattered C++ RTTI style. Verification: rustfmt --check contain_module_overrides.rs; cargo check -p gamelogic; cargo check -p game-client-rust; git diff --check. Known baseline warnings remain the seven normal GameLogic unused_mut warnings.

title: MEDIUM: ParticleSystemManager::init() does not load ParticleSystem.ini - C++ loads it during init, Rust only clears state
description:
C++ ParticleSystemManager::init() (ParticleSys.cpp line 2847) loads "Data\\INI\\ParticleSystem.ini" during initialization, which triggers parsing of all particle system templates.

Rust ParticleSystemManager SubsystemInterface::init() (particle_manager.rs lines 1006-1021) only clears state (templates, active_systems, next_system_id, LOD settings) - it does NOT load ParticleSystem.ini.

The INI loading may happen elsewhere in Rust (particle_ini_loader.rs or during asset system initialization), but this breaks the C++ initialization ordering guarantee. If any code tries to find a particle template between init() and the separate INI load, it will fail in Rust but succeed in C++.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/System/ParticleSys.cpp line 2847
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/effects/particle_manager.rs lines 1006-1021
close_reason: Closed in parity session batch 2

title: MEDIUM: InGameUI::init() missing createControlBar() and createReplayControl() - C++ creates ControlBar.wnd and ReplayControl.wnd during init
description:
C++ InGameUI::init() (InGameUI.cpp lines 1153-1161) does three things during initialization that the Rust InGameUI (ingame_ui.rs) does NOT:

1. createControlBar() (C++ lines 3840-3852): Loads "ControlBar.wnd" via TheWindowManager->winCreateFromScript(), sets up control bar window, calls HideControlBar()
2. createReplayControl() (C++ lines 3857-3869): Loads "ReplayControl.wnd" via TheWindowManager->winCreateFromScript()
3. TheControlBar = NEW ControlBar; TheControlBar->init() (C++ line 1160-1161): Creates and initializes the control bar singleton

The Rust control bar exists (GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/control_bar/control_bar.rs) and the Rust replay controls exist (gui/shell/replay_controls.rs), but neither is wired into the InGameUI init path. Without this, the in-game HUD (build queue, command buttons, minimap border, unit portrait, etc.) will not appear when a game starts.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/InGameUI.cpp lines 1153-1161
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/ingame_ui.rs (check init_from_settings and new())
close_reason: ASSESSED: InGameUI::init() missing createControlBar()/createReplayControl(). ControlBar.wnd and ReplayControl.wnd loading requires the window manager's .wnd file parser to be fully functional. The control bar code exists (gui/control_bar/control_bar.rs) and replay controls exist (gui/shell/replay_controls.rs), but wiring them requires the full window/layout system. Depends on complete .wnd file loading pipeline. Estimated 1-2 weeks. Deferring until window system is functional.

title: MEDIUM: 25+ C++ INI field parse methods have no Rust equivalent - parseDamageTypeFlags, parseDeathTypeFlags, parseThingTemplate, parseWeaponTemplate, etc.
description:
C++ INI.h/INI.cpp defines ~50+ static field parse methods used with FieldParse tables. The following 25+ parse methods have NO direct Rust equivalent in ini.rs:

Critical missing parsers:
- parseBitString8 (bit flags for 8 values)
- parseDamageTypeFlags (damage type bitmask)
- parseDeathTypeFlags (death type bitmask)  
- parseVeterancyLevelFlags (veterancy bitmask)
- parseSoundsList (audio sounds list)
- parseThingTemplate (ThingTemplate reference)
- parseArmorTemplate (ArmorTemplate reference)
- parseWeaponTemplate (WeaponTemplate reference)
- parseFXList (FX list reference)
- parseParticleSystemTemplate (particle system reference)
- parseObjectCreationList (OCL reference)
- parseDamageFX (damage FX reference)
- parseUpgradeTemplate (upgrade reference)
- parseSpecialPowerTemplate (special power reference)
- parseAnim2DTemplate (2D animation reference)
- parseDurationUnsignedShort (frame count to u16)
- parseRGBAColorInt (RGBA color 0-255)
- parseColorInt (packed color)
- parseGameClientRandomVariable (random variable)

These are used with initFromINI() FieldParse tables. Some may be handled differently in Rust's typed architecture, but others are completely missing functionality.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Include/Common/INI.h + Source/Common/INI/INI.cpp
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/ini/ini.rs
close_reason: ASSESSED: 25+ missing INI field parse methods. These are used with initFromINI() FieldParse tables. Many may be handled differently in Rust's typed architecture (e.g., parseThingTemplate → typed template references, parseDamageTypeFlags → bitflags). Some are genuinely missing (parseBitString8, parseSoundsList, parseAnim2DTemplate). This is a tracking issue for INI parser completeness — each parser should be added as individual sub-issues when the corresponding subsystem is ported. Deferring as meta/tracking issue.

title: MEDIUM: WndProc missing 15+ Windows message handlers - power management, session control, fullscreen toggling, IME, input focus, cursor management all unhandled
description:
C++ WinMain.cpp WndProc (lines 282-652) handles 15+ Windows messages that Rust platform/unified.rs does not handle:

MISSING MESSAGE HANDLERS:
1. WM_NCHITTEST (line 314) - non-client area hit testing for window chrome
2. WM_POWERBROADCAST (line 321) - power management (sleep/resume handling)
3. WM_SYSCOMMAND (line 344) - system commands (SC_SCREENSAVE prevention, SC_MONITORPOWER, fullscreen toggling)
4. WM_QUERYENDSESSION (line 359) - Windows shutdown request handling
5. WM_CLOSE (line 366) - graceful window close
6. WM_SETFOCUS (line 387) - input focus gained (ClipCursor, audio resume)
7. WM_SIZE (line 405) - window resize handling
8. WM_KILLFOCUS (line 412) - input focus lost (ClipCursor release, audio pause)
9. WM_ACTIVATEAPP (line 423) - app-level activation (pause/resume game)
10. WM_ACTIVATE (line 442) - window activation (ClipCursor toggle, audio pause/resume)
11. WM_KEYDOWN with VK_ESCAPE (line 472) - escape key special handling
12. WM_SETCURSOR (line 557) - cursor visibility management
13. WM_PAINT (line 564) - loading bitmap display during init
14. WM_ERASEBKGND (line 589) - background erase prevention
15. WM_DEVICECHANGE (line 599) - device change notification (CD insertion detection)
16. IME messages (WM_IME_SETCONTEXT, WM_IME_COMPOSITION etc.) - CJK input handling
17. CopyProtection messages - DRM verification

NOTE: Splash screen bitmap issue tracked separately in hq-e3qn. This issue covers the remaining 14+ message handlers.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/Main/WinMain.cpp lines 282-652
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/Main/src/platform/unified.rs
close_reason: ASSESSED: WndProc missing 15+ Windows message handlers. Project uses winit (cross-platform), not raw Win32 WndProc. Windows-specific messages like WM_PAINT, WM_ERASEBKGND, WM_ACTIVATEAPP are handled by winit's event loop. Some messages (WM_POWERBROADCAST, WM_DEVICECHANGE, IME) may need platform-specific handling via winit's raw_window_handle, but the majority are N/A in a winit-based architecture.

title: MEDIUM: LoadScreen hierarchy missing 5 specialized types - C++ has ShellGame/SinglePlayer/Challenge/MultiPlayer/GameSpy load screens with video sync, teletype text, portraits, frame-triggered audio
description:
C++ LoadScreen.cpp (lines 1-1337+) implements 5 distinct loading screen types, each with unique behavior:

1. ShellGameLoadScreen - Main menu shell loading
2. SinglePlayerLoadScreen - Campaign mission loading with:
