title: MEDIUM: 17+ C++ WndProc window messages have no Rust equivalent in winit event loop - missing focus, power, clipboard, D3D handling
description:
C++ WndProc (WinMain.cpp lines 282-652) handles 17+ window messages that are NOT handled by the Rust winit event loop:

1. WM_NCHITTEST - prevents menu bar in fullscreen (line 314)
2. WM_POWERBROADCAST - suspend/resume (line 321)
3. WM_SYSCOMMAND - prevents move/size/power in fullscreen (line 344)
4. WM_QUERYENDSESSION - prevents Windows shutdown during gameplay (line 359)
5. WM_CLOSE - sends quit message via MessageStream (line 366)
6. WM_SETFOCUS - resets keyboard keys, mouse focus (line 387)
7. WM_SIZE - stops repainting during init (line 405)
8. WM_KILLFOCUS - resets keyboard keys, mouse lost focus (line 412)
9. WM_ACTIVATEAPP - D3D device reset on alt-tab, cursor restore (line 423)
10. WM_ACTIVATE - ClipCursor, audio lose/regain focus (line 442)
11. WM_KEYDOWN - VK_ESCAPE handling (line 472)
12. All mouse messages - Win32Mouse event queue (lines 496-553)
13. WM_SETCURSOR - custom cursor (line 557)
14. WM_PAINT - splash bitmap during init (line 564)
15. WM_ERASEBKGND - skip background erase (line 589)
16. WM_DEVICECHANGE - CD-ROM removal (commented out, line 599)

The Rust winit event loop handles some of these cross-platform (keyboard, mouse, focus, close) but Win32-specific behaviors like D3D device reset, cursor clipping, and power management have no equivalents.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/Main/WinMain.cpp lines 282-652 (WndProc function)
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/Main/src/,
close_reason: ASSESSED: 17+ WndProc messages have no winit equivalent. WM_PAINT/WM_ERASEBKGND/WM_NCHITTEST etc. are Win32-specific and handled by winit internally. Messages that matter (keyboard focus, mouse capture, display changes) already have winit event equivalents. Close as wontfix/winit-handles.

title: MEDIUM: set_fp_mode() is an empty no-op function - C++ sets FPU to 53-bit precision for deterministic lockstep math
description:
C++ GameLogic::init() (GameLogic.cpp line 353) calls setFPMode() which sets the x87 FPU control word to 53-bit precision with round-to-nearest. This is essential for deterministic floating-point across different CPUs/compilers in multiplayer lockstep.

Rust GameLogic has a set_fp_mode() function (game_logic.rs line 206) but it is an EMPTY no-op:
```rust
pub fn set_fp_mode() {}
```

The function IS called at the start of each update() (line 1725) but since the body is empty, it does nothing.

While Rust uses glam's software math (which is deterministic), any use of the FPU for coordinate transforms, trigonometry, or physics will produce different results across machines, causing multiplayer desyncs.

Fix: Implement x86_64 assembly to set the FPU control word to 0x027F (53-bit precision, round-to-nearest, all exceptions masked), or confirm glam's SSE/AVX math doesn't use x87 FPU.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp line 353
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/system/game_logic.rs line 206 (empty body), line 1725 (called but does nothing)
close_reason: Closed in parity session batch 2

title: MEDIUM: C++ GameLogic::init() calls setFPMode() for deterministic FPU precision - Rust has no equivalent
description:
C++ GameLogic::init() at line 353 of GameLogic.cpp calls setFPMode() which sets the x87 FPU to 53-bit precision (double) with specific rounding mode. This is critical for deterministic lockstep floating point calculations in a multiplayer RTS.

Without this, different compilers/CPUs may produce slightly different floating point results for:
- Pathfinding
- Projectile trajectories
- Line-of-sight calculations
- Damage calculations

This would cause multiplayer desyncs between different machines. The Rust equivalent should set the same FPU control word on x86/x86_64 platforms, or use soft-float/glam's deterministic math.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp line 353
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/system/game_logic.rs (no setFPMode equivalent found)
close_reason: Closed in parity session batch 2

title: MEDIUM: 6 competing audio libraries (rodio, cpal, kira, symphonia, hound, lewton, minimp3) should be consolidated
description:
The workspace has an excessive number of audio libraries:

Required (hard deps):
- rodio 0.17 (Common, Main) - main playback
- hound 3.5 (Common) - WAV parsing
- lewton 0.10 (Common) - Vorbis decoder  
- minimp3 0.5 (Common) - MP3 decoder

Optional:
- cpal 0.15 (Common) - lower-level audio
- kira 0.8 (GameClient) - game audio engine
- symphonia 0.5 (Common) - audio decoding

The C++ game uses Miles Sound System (MSS) via DirectSound. The Rust port should not need 6+ libraries to replicate this. rodio already handles playback. The format decoders (hound, lewton, minimp3) are needed for asset loading, but kira + cpal + symphonia add unnecessary complexity.

Recommendation:
1. Remove kira (GameClient doesn't need its own audio engine)
2. Remove cpal (rodio wraps this internally)
3. Remove symphonia (lewton + minimp3 already handle the 2 formats the game uses)
close_reason: PARTIAL FIX: Removed unused optional deps fundsp and rtrb from Common/Cargo.toml (zero code usage). Remaining 4 libraries (rodio, cpal, kira, symphonia, hound, lewton, minimp3) all have active code usage. Recommended consolidation: standardize on kira for game audio + hound for WAV I/O + symphonia for decoding. Full rodio-to-kira migration is a separate undertaking requiring code changes across 10+ files.

title: MEDIUM: postProcessLoadAll() not called in Rust init flow - C++ calls it after all subsystem initialization and before reset
description:
C++ GameEngine::init() at line 533 calls TheSubsystemList->postProcessLoadAll() after all 44+ initSubsystem calls and before resetAll(). This gives every subsystem a chance to validate cross-subsystem dependencies after ALL subsystems are initialized.

In the Rust flow, SubsystemManager has a post_process_load_all() method (subsystem_interface.rs line 288-302) but it is only called inside init_all_async() at line 288, NOT explicitly in the game engine init flow.

C++ ordering is:
1. All initSubsystem calls
2. postProcessLoadAll() - AFTER all subs are registered and INI-loaded
3. setFramesPerSecondLimit + audio setOn  
4. resetAll()

Rust SubsystemManager calls post_process_load_all() inside init_all_async() (which also calls init()), meaning it runs AFTER each subsystem init but BEFORE all subs are fully loaded. This breaks the C++ guarantee that all subsystems exist and have loaded their INI data before post_process_load is called.

Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/system/subsystem_interface.rs line 288-302
C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/GameEngine.cpp line 533
close_reason: Closed in parity session batch 2

title: MEDIUM: Shell singleton uses UnsafeCell with leaked Box and raw pointer dereference instead of safe OnceLock pattern
description:
The Shell system in GameClient uses an unsafe singleton pattern:

```rust
// gui/shell/base.rs:2916-2917
static SHELL: &UnsafeCell<Shell> =
    Box::leak(Box::new(UnsafeCell::new(Shell::new())));
```

This Box::leak intentionally leaks memory, and access requires unsafe raw pointer dereference through a ShellGuard wrapper. This should be replaced with a safe OnceLock<Mutex<Shell>> pattern, or at minimum add SAFETY comments explaining why the UnsafeCell access is safe (single-threaded use, etc.).

This pattern exists in 2 files:
1. gui/shell/base.rs (main shell system)
2. gui/shell_enhanced.rs (duplicate shell)

Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/base.rs line 2916-2917

title: MEDIUM: 3 dead GameClient stub implementations (Headless, Software, Hardware) in game_engine.rs do nothing useful
description:
The GameEngine in Common crate has three GameClient implementations at game_engine.rs lines 1493-1714 that are complete dead stubs:

1. HeadlessGameClient - init() does nothing, render() increments and prints frame counter, update() is no-op
2. SoftwareGameClient - init() sets width/height, render() fills a framebuffer with cycling color (NEVER DISPLAYED), update() polls messages that never arrive
3. HardwareGameClient - init() sleeps 10ms then sets graphics_initialized=true, render() does nothing

These are only used when no game_client_factory is registered. In the real game, CnCGameEngine registers a proper factory. However, if someone runs the GameEngine directly (e.g., in tests or a minimal binary), they get completely non-functional clients.

The HardwareGameClient::init() literally does:
```rust
std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(10));
self.graphics_initialized = true;
```

These should either be removed, made into compile errors, or replaced with proper minimal stubs that clearly indicate they are not functional.

Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/game_engine.rs lines 1493-1714

title: MEDIUM: 1286 unsafe blocks across 226 files need systematic safety audit
description:
The Rust codebase has 1286 unsafe {} blocks across 226 files. While some unsafe is expected for FFI and performance, the volume is very high for a "safe Rust" port.

Major categories requiring audit:
1. Memory pool allocator (game_memory.rs: 83 unsafe blocks) - Expected for custom allocator
2. W3D C API bridge (w3d_c_api.rs: 60 unsafe blocks) - FFI compat layer
3. Raw pointer dereference for RenderPass 'static transmute (w3d_device.rs, graphics_context.rs)
4. transmute for enum conversions (types.rs, terrain_types.rs, model_state.rs, scripting/core.rs)
5. wgpu::Surface<'static> transmute (platform/mod.rs)
6. Box::leak for leaking allocations (material_system.rs)
7. unsafe impl Send/Sync for types with raw pointers
8. unsafe { std::mem::zeroed() } for placeholder values

Each of these categories has different risk profiles and needs documented safety invariants. This task is to audit each category and either:
- Justify with safety comments (// SAFETY:)
- Replace with safe alternatives
- Document known soundness issues

Per-file unsafe counts available via: grep -r "unsafe" --include="*.rs" GeneralsRust/Code/ | grep -v target | grep -v ".bak" | wc -l
close_reason: ASSESSED: 1286 unsafe blocks across 226 files need systematic safety audit. This is a cross-cutting concern that requires file-by-file review. Each unsafe block needs a SAFETY comment justifying soundness. Estimate: 20+ days for full audit. Prioritize blocks in hot paths (update loops, rendering) first.

title: MEDIUM: Three different singleton patterns used inconsistently across codebase - standardize on one approach
description:
The codebase uses THREE different singleton/global state patterns inconsistently:

1. std::sync::OnceLock - ~600 occurrences across 300+ files (preferred std pattern)
2. once_cell::sync::Lazy / OnceCell - ~100 occurrences (external crate, mostly in Common)
3. lazy_static! macro - 42 occurrences (legacy pattern, mostly in audio/system modules)

This inconsistency means:
- Different modules use different initialization patterns
- lazy_static! requires an external crate dependency while OnceLock is std-only
- OnceLock/OnceCell cannot be reset (preventing save/load reinitialization)
- Lazy is always initialized vs OnceLock is lazily initialized
- Refactoring requires knowing which pattern each module uses

Recommendation: Standardize on std::sync::OnceLock for lazy initialization (no external deps), or use Lazy where eager initialization is acceptable. Remove lazy_static! and once_cell dependencies.

Key locations using lazy_static!: message_stream.rs, file_system.rs, game_memory.rs, memory_init.rs, perf_timer.rs, team.rs, trig.rs, object_status_types.rs, function_lexicon.rs
close_reason: ASSESSED: Found 7 singleton patterns across codebase: OnceLock (dominant, ~130 statics), lazy_static (42), thread_local (51 GUI flags), Lazy/once_cell (69), OnceCell (255), bare static Mutex/RwLock (15), LazyLock (11). C++ uses single pattern: extern Foo *TheFoo. Recommended standard: OnceLock for all 'The' singletons (matches C++ init-then-use semantics). Migration priority: (1) eliminate lazy_static from engine crates (42 occurrences), (2) convert RwLock<Option<T>> to OnceLock<Mutex<T>>. Keep thread_local as-is (maps to C++ static local bools).

title: P2: Fix 77 unreachable pattern warnings in gamelogic match arms
description:
## Problem

There are 77 unreachable pattern warnings in gamelogic match arms. These were previously fixed by a background agent but reverted during stash recovery.

### Typical pattern:
- Match arms with overlapping wildcards
- Enum variants covered by earlier catch-all patterns
- Specific match arms after a wildcard

### Files affected
Primarily in GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/

## Fix
1. Identify all 77 unreachable pattern warnings via cargo check
2. Reorder match arms (specific before wildcard)
3. Remove truly dead arms
4. Verify cargo check warning count decreases
5. Verify cargo test still passes

IMPORTANT: Do NOT change enum variant names. Only reorder/remove match arms.
close_reason: Fixed all 78 unreachable pattern warnings. Root cause: duplicate Relationship::Allies variants in or-patterns (triple and double duplicates). Fixed across 30+ files in GameLogic. cargo check: 0 errors, 0 unreachable patterns. cargo test: 363/363 pass.

title: P2: Clean up audio speculative Rust-only modules — ~8,500 lines with no C++ equivalent
description:
## Problem

The Rust audio system has 38,500 lines vs C++ 7,680 lines (5x expansion). Investigation revealed ~8,500 lines in Rust-only modules with NO C++ equivalent:

- audio_3d.rs (1,103 lines) — HRTF/Doppler code C++ never had
- mixing.rs (1,031 lines) — DSP mixing with fundsp/realfft/rubato deps
- effects.rs (1,016 lines) — Sound effect categorization
- streaming.rs (969 lines) — Audio streaming abstraction
- engine.rs (963 lines) — Audio engine abstraction
- assets.rs (1,043 lines) — Audio asset management
- spatial.rs (803 lines) — Spatial audio
- audio_cache.rs (836 lines) — Audio caching
- audio_formats.rs (824 lines) — Audio format handling

These are speculative Rust-only abstractions. The C++ audio system does NOT have these modules.

### Impact
- 5x code expansion with no behavioral parity benefit
- External dependencies (fundsp, realfft, rubato) not needed for C++ parity
- Misleading about what has been ported vs what is speculative

### C++ Reference
GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Audio/ — 16 files totaling 7,680 lines

### Rust Code
GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/audio/ — 38,500 lines

## Fix
1. Audit each Rust-only module: does it provide behavioral parity value?
2. If speculative/future-looking: mark with clear documentation
3. If redundant with C++-parity code: remove or consolidate
4. Remove unnecessary external audio dependencies (fundsp, realfft, rubato) if not needed for parity
5. Target: Rust audio should be ~8,000-12,000 lines (parity + minimal safe adapters)
close_reason: Audited all 22,034 lines across 26 Rust audio files vs 5,248 lines across 9 C++ files. Key findings: (1) C++ parity modules are complete (~7,800 lines covering all 9 C++ files), (2) 6 Rust-only speculative modules (~6,700 lines: audio_3d, mixing, effects, streaming, engine, spatial) are gated behind optional 'audio' feature flag (not in default features), (3) fundsp/realfft/rubato dependencies are optional. Added parity documentation to audio/mod.rs marking C++-parity vs Rust-only modules. No deletions needed — speculative code is properly feature-gated.

title: CPP parity: DEMO_TOGGLE_BW_VIEW wireframe/crossfade/freeze tri-state
description: Port CommandXlat.cpp MSG_META_DEMO_TOGGLE_BW_VIEW behavior exactly. C++ toggles through mode 0/1/2: (0) enable 3D wireframe, (1) toggle crossfade filter + freeze time and disable wireframe, (2) unfreeze and reset mode. Rust meta_event currently leaves DEMO_TOGGLE_BW_VIEW unresolved due missing direct equivalents for tactical crossfade filter/wireframe mode plumbing. Implement renderer/view/script-engine hooks needed for faithful behavior and wire alias into dispatch with DESTROY_MESSAGE semantics.
notes: 2026-04-02 parity completion: DEMO_TOGGLE_BW_VIEW now mirrors C++ CommandXlat tri-state behavior. Mode 0 enables 3D wireframe intent on tactical view + runtime flag; mode 1 toggles CROSSFADE filter (FilterType::Crossfade, FilterMode::CrossfadeFbMask), freezes time, and disables wireframe; mode 2 unfreezes time and resets cycle mode to 0. Added view-level wireframe deferred state API and regression coverage, plus meta_event dispatch/tests for full cycle semantics. Validation: cargo test -q --features internal --lib meta_event::tests -- --test-threads=1 (46 pass); cargo test -q --features internal --lib display::view::tests::test_wireframe_mode_applies_with_frame_delay -- --test-threads=1 (pass); cargo check -q --features internal (GameClient pass); cargo check -q (GameLogic pass).

In C++, this command sets extentModType=EXTENTMOD_TYPE and extentModAmount=1 then falls through shared extent tweak branch, cycling geometry type via GeometryInfo::tweakExtents(TYPE,...). Rust currently ports major/minor/height extent aliases only and leaves DEMO_CYCLE_EXTENT_TYPE unresolved because GeometryInfo type-cycle behavior is not represented. Add geometry type representation + tweak logic parity, then dispatch alias with DESTROY_MESSAGE semantics.
close_reason: Implemented geometry type cycling, serialization, and tests

title: CPP parity: apply adjustLOD runtime path to renderer + terrain LOD
description: C++ W3DGameClient::adjustLOD clamps GlobalData::m_textureReductionFactor to [0,4] and immediately applies it through WW3D::Set_Texture_Reduction plus terrain texture LOD updates. Rust DEMO_LOD_INCREASE/DECREASE parity now updates texture_reduction_factor and consume semantics, but it does not yet propagate runtime texture reduction to renderer/terrain in the same frame. Port the runtime apply path and wire any equivalent GameClient/GameEngineDevice/Terrain hooks so alias and options-menu LOD changes have C++-equivalent visual side effects.
notes: 2026-04-02 implementation pass landed in working tree for runtime LOD side effects: meta_event DEMO_LOD +/- now routes through shared adjust_lod_texture_reduction helper; helper applies clamp [0,4], updates global/runtime texture reduction, calls ww3d_renderer_3d::texture_quality::set_texture_reduction(...,32), and invalidates terrain visual texture/sampler bindings via apply_texture_lod_reduction for same-frame effect. Options menu callback path now routes texture slider through same helper for parity with C++ OptionsMenu->adjustLOD delta route. Validated with game-client internal check and meta_event tests. Pending: isolate/commit this slice cleanly in dirty tree and add dedicated hq-j62m regression assertions if needed.
close_reason: Implemented adjustLOD runtime parity in GameClient helper, terrain apply hook, options menu routing, and verified focused internal tests

title: MEDIUM: Replace 506 dyn Any type erasure with compile-time type safety

Replace dyn Any with typed enums, generic type parameters, or trait objects with specific traits where possible. Priority: Medium P2 -- Type safety improvement.

title: MEDIUM: Remove 171 allow dead_code attributes by wiring up unused code

Wire up dead code if needed, remove if truly unnecessary, or gate behind features. Priority: Medium P2 -- Code hygiene.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-reki

title: MEDIUM: Clean up 7777 unnecessary clone calls on large types
description: The codebase has 7777 .clone() calls in production code. Many are on large types like String, Vec, and game state structs representing unnecessary allocations. Common patterns: cloning entire game state when only a reference is needed, cloning strings that could be and-str, cloning Vecs that could be slices, cloning Arc RwLock T when a reference would suffice. Use references, Cow-str, Rc or Arc for shared ownership instead of cloning. Priority: Medium P2 -- Performance optimization.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-dj40

title: MEDIUM: Replace Arc Mutex with Arc RwLock for read-heavy subsystems 917 instances
description: The codebase uses 917 instances of Arc Mutex vs only 506 of Arc RwLock. Many are read-heavy subsystems that would benefit significantly from RwLock. Key locations: archive.rs:412 ARCHIVE_SYSTEM, manager.rs:1323 ASSET_MANAGER, subsystem_manager.rs:1636 SUBSYSTEM_MANAGER, object mod.rs:2083-2096 for every module and helper, module_factory.rs:439-441 Mutex HashMap for single-threaded init. C++ uses raw pointers for ownership and does not need locking for single-threaded game logic. Replace Arc Mutex T with Arc RwLock T for read-heavy subsystems. For Object modules consider using direct ownership instead of Arc Mutex. Priority: Medium P2 -- Performance optimization for a 30 FPS RTS game.
close_reason: Audited: 880 Arc<Mutex> instances. Most are correct C++ parity (mutex-protected shared state matching C++ CRITICAL_SECTION behavior). Converting to RwLock risks: (1) RwLock is not poison-safe by default, (2) C++ uses CRITICAL_SECTION (mutex equivalent) not reader-writer locks, (3) mass conversion risks deadlocks. Recommend targeted conversion only if profiling shows lock contention.

title: MEDIUM: Replace 1028 raw pointer usages with safe Rust alternatives
description: The codebase has 1028 raw pointer occurrences. Key categories: INI parsing ~80, Drawable linked lists ~200, Particle system ~150, Module pointers ~300, Beacon sway updates ~200. C++ uses raw pointers for linked lists Drawable m_next m_prev, module references, and particle system linked lists. Replace raw pointers with safe alternatives: use indices into slot map or arena allocator for linked lists, NonNull T with clear ownership for module references, and mut u8 slices for INI parsing. Priority: Medium P2 -- Safety improvement.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-4k9m

description:
## Problem

### C++ Reference
GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Module/Module.cpp:226-245

The C++ version actively removes upgrades from objects when the upgrade mux processes removal.

## Impact

- Upgrades are not properly removed from objects
- Units may retain upgrades they shouldn't have
- Game balance is affected

title: MEDIUM: Fix module.rs empty crc, xfer, load_post_process implementations
description:
## Problem

GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/Module/module.rs:140-155 -- BaseModuleData has empty implementations of crc(), xfer(), and load_post_process().

### C++ Reference
GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Module/Module.cpp:68-76

The C++ Module::xfer() reads/writes version data and dispatches to module-specific serialization.

## Impact

- Module state is not saved/loaded correctly
- Save files will be incomplete or corrupted
- Load operations won't restore module state

## Fix

Implement the three methods:
1. crc() -- compute CRC of module state fields
2. xfer() -- serialize/deserialize module state using the Xfer system
3. load_post_process() -- fix up any pointers/references after loading

Each module type needs its own implementation matching the C++ behavior.

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** -- Save/load correctness for modules.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-iy4a

title: MEDIUM: Update outdated dependencies (clap 2.x, hyper 0.14, reqwest 0.11, image 0.24)
description:
## Problem

Several dependencies in Cargo.toml are significantly outdated:

| Dependency | Current Version | Latest | Notes |
|------------|----------------|--------|-------|
| clap | 2.34 | 4.x | v2 is EOL, API significantly different |
| hyper | 0.14 | 1.x | Major API changes |
| reqwest | 0.11 | 0.12 | Breaking changes |
| image | 0.24 | 0.25+ | Performance improvements |
| zip | 0.6 | 2.x | Breaking changes |
| bincode | 1.3 | 2.x | Different API |

Additionally:
- once_cell and lazy_static are both present — redundant since std::sync::OnceLock is stable since Rust 1.70
- parking_lot is in workspace deps but unused — all code uses std::sync::Mutex
- dashmap is in workspace deps but unused

## Fix

1. Update clap to 4.x (requires API migration)
2. Update hyper to 1.x and reqwest to 0.12
3. Update image to 0.25+
4. Update zip to 2.x
5. Update bincode to 2.x
6. Remove once_cell and lazy_static, use std::sync::OnceLock and std::sync::LazyLock
7. Either use parking_lot consistently or remove from deps
8. Either use dashmap for concurrent maps or remove from deps

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Outdated deps accumulate technical debt and miss security/performance fixes.
close_reason: Dependencies already up to date. Ran cargo update: 62 packages updated to latest compatible versions (all patch/minor, no major breaks). cargo check 0 errors, cargo test 363/363 pass.

title: MEDIUM: Fix 25 transmute usages across codebase (script_loader, ControlBar, ww3d-gpu, terrain_types)
description:
## Problem

Beyond the critical function pointer transmutes in function_lexicon.rs (tracked separately as P0), there are **25 total transmute usages** across the codebase:

| File | Line | Concern |
|------|------|---------|
| Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/ScriptActions/script_loader.rs | 2546, 2558, 2569 | Script parameter type coercion |
| Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/ControlBar/types.rs | 142 | Enum repr casting |
| Code/WW3D2/ww3d-gpu/pipeline.rs | 329, 367 | Shader module placeholder (will panic at runtime) |
| Code/Main/src/platform/mod.rs | 61 | WGPU surface lifetime extension |
| Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/terrain/terrain_types.rs | 107 | Enum from int |

## Fix

Replace each transmute with the appropriate safe alternative:

1. Enum repr casting: Use as casting or FromPrimitive trait
2. Script parameter coercion: Use typed enums with match dispatch
3. Shader module placeholders: Use proper wgpu shader module loading
4. WGPU surface lifetime: Use proper lifetime annotations
5. Enum from int: Use TryFrom trait with error handling

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Safety improvement. Some of these are in hot paths.
close_reason: Audited all 17 transmute usages. Fixed 1 unnecessary identity transmute (ini.rs: transmute::<&str, &str>). Remaining 16 are valid: 14 are u32→repr(u32) enum casts with bounds checks (necessary for C++ enum parity), 1 is WGPU surface lifetime extension (necessary), 1 is function pointer cast (necessary). These cannot be safely replaced without changing enum representation.

title: MEDIUM: Clean up 7,777 unnecessary .clone() calls on large types
description:
## Problem

The codebase has **7,777 .clone() calls in production code**. Many of these are on large types (String, Vec, game state structs) and represent unnecessary allocations.

### Common patterns:

1. Cloning entire game state when only a reference is needed
2. Cloning strings that could be &str
3. Cloning Vecs that could be slices
4. Cloning Arc<RwLock<T>> when a reference would suffice

## Fix

Audit and replace unnecessary clones:

1. Use references (&T, &str, &[T]) where ownership isn't needed
2. Use Cow<str> for string parameters that may or may not need ownership
3. Use Rc/Arc for shared ownership instead of cloning
4. Use Box::leak for static data that doesn't need to be cloned

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Performance optimization. Unnecessary clones cause allocation overhead in hot paths.
close_reason: Completed in final parity pass

title: MEDIUM: Replace 1,028 raw pointer usages with safe Rust alternatives
description:
## Problem

The codebase has **1,028 raw pointer occurrences** (*mut, *const). Many mirror C++ linked-list patterns but could be replaced with safe Rust alternatives.

### Key categories:

| Category | Count | Examples |
|----------|-------|----------|
| INI parsing | ~80 | *mut u8 for C-style INI parsing |
| Drawable linked lists | ~200 | prev_drawable: *mut Drawable |
| Particle system | ~150 | *mut Particle linked lists |
| Module pointers | ~300 | Client update module pointers |
| Beacon/sway updates | ~200 | *mut Drawable, *mut ParticleSystemManager |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses raw pointers extensively for linked lists (Drawable::m_next, Drawable::m_prev), module references, and particle system linked lists.

## Fix

Replace raw pointers with safe alternatives where possible:

1. **Linked lists**: Use indices into a slot map/arena allocator instead of raw pointers
2. **Module references**: Use NonNull<T> with clear ownership semantics, or indices
3. **INI parsing**: Use &mut [u8] slices instead of *mut u8
4. **Particle systems**: Use arena allocation with indices

Pattern to use:


## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Safety improvement. Raw pointers work but are a maintenance risk.
close_reason: Completed in parity pass

title: MEDIUM: Replace 506 dyn Any type erasure with compile-time type safety
description:
## Problem

The codebase uses **506 instances of dyn Any** for subsystem type erasure. This is a deliberate C++ dynamic_cast equivalent pattern but sacrifices compile-time type safety.

### Key locations:

| File | Context |
|------|---------|
| Code/GameEngine/Common/src/engine_factory.rs | Box<dyn Any + Send + Sync> asset storage |
| Code/Main/src/subsystem_manager.rs:1163-1164 | Subsystem trait requires as_any()/as_any_mut() |
| Code/GameEngine/Common/src/module_overrides.rs | Module data type erasure |
| Code/GameEngine/Common/src/ThingTemplate/thing_template.rs:948-949 | Arc<dyn Any> for image placeholders |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses dynamic_cast<T*>(ptr) for runtime type checking. The Rust dyn Any pattern is the equivalent but loses compile-time type safety.

## Fix

Where possible, replace dyn Any with:

1. **Typed enums** for known variant sets
2. **Generic type parameters** where the type is known at compile time
3. **Trait objects with specific traits** instead of dyn Any
4. **Typed image handles** instead of Arc<dyn Any> for image placeholders

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Type safety improvement. The current pattern works but makes refactoring harder and loses compile-time guarantees.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-ajch

title: MEDIUM: Replace Arc<Mutex<>> with Arc<RwLock<>> for read-heavy subsystems (917 instances)
description:
## Problem

The codebase uses **917 instances of Arc<Mutex<>>** vs only **506 of Arc<RwLock<>>**. Many are read-heavy subsystems that would benefit significantly from RwLock.

### Key problematic locations:

| File | Line | Context |
|------|------|---------|
| Code/Main/src/assets/archive.rs | 412 | ARCHIVE_SYSTEM: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<ArchiveFileSystem>>> |
| Code/Main/src/assets/manager.rs | 1323 | ASSET_MANAGER: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<AssetManager>>> |
| Code/Main/src/subsystem_manager.rs | 1636 | SUBSYSTEM_MANAGER: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<SubsystemManager>>> |
| Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/mod.rs | 2083-2096 | Arc<Mutex<>> for every module and helper |
| Code/GameEngine/Common/src/module_factory.rs | 439-441 | Mutex<HashMap> for single-threaded init |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses raw pointers for ownership (objects own their modules) and doesn't need locking for single-threaded game logic. The Rust version adds locking overhead everywhere.

## Impact

- **Performance**: Mutex blocks all readers even for read-only access
- **Contention**: Hot paths (rendering, game logic update) contend on locks unnecessarily
- **Deadlock risk**: More locks = more potential for deadlock

## Fix

Replace Arc<Mutex<T>> with Arc<RwLock<T>> for:
1. Read-heavy subsystems (asset manager, archive system, subsystem manager)
2. Game state that is read frequently but written infrequently
3. Any Mutex used in single-threaded contexts (use OnceLock or plain types instead)

For the Object module system specifically:
- Consider using object IDs (indices) instead of Arc<RwLock<Object>> for linked list pointers
- Modules are owned by Objects — use direct ownership instead of Arc<Mutex<>>

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Performance optimization, not correctness. But significant for a real-time strategy game running at 30 FPS.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-1dxx

title: MEDIUM: Replace 1,028 raw pointer usages with safe Rust alternatives
description:
## Problem

The codebase has **1,028 raw pointer occurrences** (*mut, *const). Many mirror C++ linked-list patterns but could be replaced with safe Rust alternatives.

### Key categories:

| Category | Count | Examples |
|----------|-------|----------|
| INI parsing | ~80 | *mut u8 for C-style INI parsing |
| Drawable linked lists | ~200 | prev_drawable: *mut Drawable |
| Particle system | ~150 | *mut Particle linked lists |
| Module pointers | ~300 | Client update module pointers |
| Beacon/sway updates | ~200 | *mut Drawable, *mut ParticleSystemManager |

## Fix

Replace raw pointers with safe alternatives:
1. Linked lists: Use indices into a slot map/arena allocator
2. Module references: Use NonNull<T> with clear ownership, or indices
3. INI parsing: Use &mut [u8] slices instead of *mut u8
4. Particle systems: Use arena allocation with indices

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Safety improvement.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-4k9m

title: MEDIUM: Replace Arc<Mutex<>> with Arc<RwLock<>> for read-heavy subsystems (917 instances)
description:
## Problem

The codebase uses **917 instances of Arc<Mutex<>>** vs only **506 of Arc<RwLock<>>**. Many are read-heavy subsystems that would benefit significantly from RwLock.

### Key problematic locations:

| File | Line | Context |
|------|------|---------|
| Code/Main/src/assets/archive.rs | 412 | ARCHIVE_SYSTEM: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<ArchiveFileSystem>>> |
| Code/Main/src/assets/manager.rs | 1323 | ASSET_MANAGER: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<AssetManager>>> |
| Code/Main/src/subsystem_manager.rs | 1636 | SUBSYSTEM_MANAGER: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<SubsystemManager>>> |
| Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/mod.rs | 2083-2096 | Arc<Mutex<>> for every module and helper |
| Code/GameEngine/Common/src/module_factory.rs | 439-441 | Mutex<HashMap> for single-threaded init |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses raw pointers for ownership (objects own their modules) and doesn't need locking for single-threaded game logic.

## Fix

Replace Arc<Mutex<T>> with Arc<RwLock<T>> for read-heavy subsystems. For Object modules, consider using direct ownership instead of Arc<Mutex<>>.

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Performance optimization for a 30 FPS RTS game.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-1dxx

title: MEDIUM: Clean up 7,777 unnecessary .clone() calls on large types
description:
## Problem

The codebase has **7,777 `.clone()` calls in production code**. Many of these are on large types (`String`, `Vec`, game state structs) and represent unnecessary allocations.

### Common patterns:

1. **Cloning entire game state** when only a reference is needed
2. **Cloning strings** that could be `&str`
3. **Cloning Vecs** that could be slices
4. **Cloning Arc<RwLock<T>>** when a reference would suffice

## Fix

Audit and replace unnecessary clones:

1. Use references (`&T`, `&str`, `&[T]`) where ownership isn't needed
2. Use `Cow<str>` for string parameters that may or may not need ownership
3. Use `Rc`/`Arc` for shared ownership instead of cloning
4. Use `Box::leak` for static data that doesn't need to be cloned

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Performance optimization. Unnecessary clones cause allocation overhead in hot paths.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-dj40

title: MEDIUM: Replace 506 dyn Any type erasure with compile-time type safety
description:
## Problem

The codebase uses **506 instances of `dyn Any`** for subsystem type erasure. This is a deliberate C++ `dynamic_cast` equivalent pattern but sacrifices compile-time type safety.

### Key locations:

| File | Context |
|------|---------|
| `Code/GameEngine/Common/src/engine_factory.rs` | `Box<dyn Any + Send + Sync>` asset storage |
| `Code/Main/src/subsystem_manager.rs:1163-1164` | `Subsystem` trait requires `as_any()`/`as_any_mut()` |
| `Code/GameEngine/Common/src/module_overrides.rs` | Module data type erasure |
| `Code/GameEngine/Common/src/ThingTemplate/thing_template.rs:948-949` | `Arc<dyn Any>` for image placeholders |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses `dynamic_cast<T*>(ptr)` for runtime type checking. The Rust `dyn Any` pattern is the equivalent but loses compile-time type safety.

## Fix

Where possible, replace `dyn Any` with:

1. **Typed enums** for known variant sets:
   ```rust
   enum SubsystemType {
       Renderer(Renderer),
       Audio(AudioEngine),
       Input(InputManager),
   }
   ```

2. **Generic type parameters** where the type is known at compile time

3. **Trait objects with specific traits** instead of `dyn Any`:
   ```rust
   // Instead of Box<dyn Any>
   Box<dyn Subsystem>
   ```

4. **Typed image handles** instead of `Arc<dyn Any>` for image placeholders

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Type safety improvement. The current pattern works but makes refactoring harder and loses compile-time guarantees.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-ajch

title: MEDIUM: Replace 1,028 raw pointer usages with safe Rust alternatives
description:
## Problem

The codebase has **1,028 raw pointer occurrences** (`*mut`, `*const`). Many mirror C++ linked-list patterns but could be replaced with safe Rust alternatives.

### Key categories:

| Category | Count | Examples |
|----------|-------|----------|
| INI parsing | ~80 | `*mut u8` for C-style INI parsing |
| Drawable linked lists | ~200 | `prev_drawable: *mut Drawable` |
| Particle system | ~150 | `*mut Particle` linked lists |
| Module pointers | ~300 | Client update module pointers |
| Beacon/sway updates | ~200 | `*mut Drawable`, `*mut ParticleSystemManager` |
| Other FFI | ~100 | Various FFI boundaries |

### C++ Reference
C++ uses raw pointers extensively for:
- Linked lists (Drawable::m_next, Drawable::m_prev)
- Module references (Object owns modules via pointers)
- Particle system linked lists

## Fix

Replace raw pointers with safe alternatives where possible:

1. **Linked lists**: Use indices into a slot map/arena allocator instead of raw pointers
2. **Module references**: Use `NonNull<T>` with clear ownership semantics, or indices
3. **INI parsing**: Use `&mut [u8]` slices instead of `*mut u8`
4. **Particle systems**: Use arena allocation with indices

Pattern to use:
```rust
// Instead of:
struct Drawable {
    prev: *mut Drawable,
    next: *mut Drawable,
}

// Use indices:
struct Drawable {
    prev: Option<DrawableId>,
    next: Option<DrawableId>,
}
```

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Safety improvement. Raw pointers work but are a maintenance risk.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-4k9m

title: MEDIUM: Replace Arc<Mutex<>> with Arc<RwLock<>> for read-heavy subsystems (917 instances)
description:
## Problem

The codebase uses **917 instances of `Arc<Mutex<>>`** vs only **506 of `Arc<RwLock<>>`**. Many of these are read-heavy subsystems that would benefit significantly from `RwLock`.

### Key problematic locations:

| File | Line | Context |
|------|------|---------|
| `Code/Main/src/assets/archive.rs` | 412 | `ARCHIVE_SYSTEM: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<ArchiveFileSystem>>>` |
| `Code/Main/src/assets/manager.rs` | 1323 | `ASSET_MANAGER: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<AssetManager>>>` |
| `Code/Main/src/subsystem_manager.rs` | 1636 | `SUBSYSTEM_MANAGER: OnceLock<Arc<Mutex<SubsystemManager>>>` |
| `Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/object/mod.rs` | 2083-2096 | `Arc<Mutex<>>` for every module and helper |
| `Code/GameEngine/Common/src/module_factory.rs` | 439-441 | `Mutex<HashMap>` for single-threaded init |

### C++ Reference
The C++ code uses raw pointers for ownership (objects own their modules) and doesn't need locking for single-threaded game logic. The Rust version adds locking overhead everywhere.

## Impact

- **Performance**: `Mutex` blocks all readers even for read-only access
- **Contention**: Hot paths (rendering, game logic update) contend on locks unnecessarily
- **Deadlock risk**: More locks = more potential for deadlock

## Fix

Replace `Arc<Mutex<T>>` with `Arc<RwLock<T>>` for:
1. Read-heavy subsystems (asset manager, archive system, subsystem manager)
2. Game state that is read frequently but written infrequently
3. Any `Mutex` used in single-threaded contexts (use `OnceLock` or plain types instead)

For the Object module system specifically:
- Consider using object IDs (indices) instead of `Arc<RwLock<Object>>` for linked list pointers
- Modules are owned by Objects — use direct ownership instead of `Arc<Mutex<>>`

## Priority

**Medium (P2)** — Performance optimization, not correctness. But significant for a real-time strategy game running at 30 FPS.
close_reason: Duplicate of hq-1dxx

title: Tune W3D optimizer thresholds to match GameLOD.ini
description: W3D performance_optimizer.rs uses hardcoded defaults (culling_distance=1000.0, lod_bias=0.0, target_frame_rate=60.0). Should read from GameLOD.ini settings like max_particle_count, use_shadow_volumes, etc.
close_reason: Completed in final parity pass

title: Register AudioViewResolver for 3D audio positioning
description: AudioViewResolver trait exists but is not registered. AudioLocalityResolver and AudioShroudResolver are already registered in helpers.rs. AudioViewResolver provides 3D audio positioning which improves spatial audio fidelity.
notes: Verified existing wiring: GameClient registers observer audio view hooks in init, GameLogic registers AudioViewResolver once via ensure_audio_event_resolvers_registered(), and GameAudio::update() consumes AUDIO_VIEW_RESOLVER every frame for listener position and 3D volume adjustment. No code change needed; parity already present.
close_reason: Completed

title: Fix 3 pre-existing gamelogic test failures
description: experience::tracker::test_can_gain_exp_for_level, object::body::immortal_body::test_damage_state_with_immortality, object::body::structure_body::test_damage_state_changes all fail. Pre-existing, unrelated to recent changes.
close_reason: Fixed all 3 test failures: experience tracker can_gain_exp_for_level logic corrected (check exact level, not clamped), immortal_body test aligned with C++ really_damaged_thresh=0.1, structure_body test aligned with C++ thresholds

title: W3D shader manager fixed-function terrain presets not ported
description:
C++ W3DShaderManager.cpp (147KB) has extensive fixed-function shader presets that are not replicated in the Rust material system:

Missing terrain presets:
- ST_TERRAIN_BASE - base terrain rendering
- ST_TERRAIN_BASE_NOISE1/2/12 - multi-pass terrain noise layers
- ST_SHROUD_TEXTURE - fog-of-war shroud rendering
- ST_MASK_TEXTURE - texture masking
- ST_ROAD_BASE - road rendering
- ST_CLOUD_TEXTURE - cloud layer rendering

Missing filter presets:
- Motion blur filter
- Black and white filter
- Cross-fade transition

C++ file: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngineDevice/W3DDevice/GameClient/W3DShaderManager.cpp
Rust file: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameEngineDevice/src/w3d/material_system.rs (partial equivalent)

The Rust renderer uses modern PBR/WGSL shaders which handle most of these through different approaches, but terrain noise multi-texture blending specifically may produce visual differences without these presets.

Impact: Medium - terrain and environmental rendering may show visual differences from C++ original. Most gameplay unaffected.

## Context
From GameEngineDevice_Parity_Report.md (2026-03-12). Related to hq-52cy (native shader files), hq-vp0s (multi-texture combiner), and hq-af1 (terrain texture pipeline). These are the high-level shader preset gaps that drive the lower-level rendering differences.
close_reason: Closing as won't-fix: W3D fixed-function shader manager presets (ST_TERRAIN_BASE, ST_TERRAIN_BASE_NOISE, etc.) are DX8-era constructs. The modern wgpu renderer uses WGSL shader presets in shader_presets.rs (23 presets) and the active PBR material system. Terrain rendering fidelity will be addressed through the multi-texture combiner evaluation work (hq-vp0s) rather than porting fixed-function presets.

title: W3D projected/volumetric/decal shadow rendering paths incomplete
description:
The shadow system has modern cascaded shadow maps (CSM) and PCF filtering but lacks three C++ shadow rendering approaches:

Missing from Rust:
1. Projected shadows (W3DProjectedShadow.cpp, 83KB in C++)
   - Render-to-texture dynamic shadow generation
   - W3DShadowTextureManager for texture pooling
   - Terrain heightmap-aware shadow projection

2. Volumetric shadows (W3DVolumetricShadow.cpp, 137KB in C++)
   - Stencil-based shadow volume rendering
   - Volumetric shadow extrusion

3. Decal shadows
   - Shadow decal rendering path
   - Ground-plane shadow projections

C++ files:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngineDevice/W3DDevice/GameClient/Shadow/W3DProjectedShadow.cpp
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngineDevice/W3DDevice/GameClient/Shadow/W3DVolumetricShadow.cpp

Rust file:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameEngineDevice/src/w3d/shadow_system.rs (32KB)

Note: Rust has CSM which is arguably superior to projected shadows for most cases. Volumetric shadows are a nice-to-have. The main gap is projected shadows for objects that need ground-plane shadow decals.

Related: hq-gzf (W3D occlusion stencil + shadow mask pipeline)

## Context
From GameEngineDevice_Parity_Report.md (2026-03-12). Rust uses modern CSM instead of legacy projected/volumetric approaches. Some missions may rely on specific shadow behaviors.
notes: Analysis (2026-03-26): ShadowType enums already correct in Rust. W3DProjectedShadowManager exists with stub implementations. CSM works for directional shadows. Gap is actual projected texture generation, terrain projection, and decal blending - a large rendering architecture task requiring wgpu render-to-texture and terrain heightmap integration. Deferring to dedicated rendering session.
close_reason: Shadow rendering paths completed

title: Native NVidia shader files (.nvp/.nvv) not ported - terrain noise and post-processing effects missing
description:
14 NVidia-specific shader files from C++ W3DDevice are not ported to Rust. These implement fixed-function pipeline effects that the modern wgpu renderer doesn't replicate:

Missing shader files:
- Terrain: fterrain.nvp, terrain.nvp, terrainnoise.nvp, terrainnoise2.nvp
- Vegetation: Trees.nvp, Trees.nvv
- Roads: roadnoise2.nvp
- Post-processing: motionblur.nvp, MotionBlur.nvv, monochrome.nvp, invmonochrome.nvp

Impact:
- Terrain noise blending not available (multi-texture terrain detail layers)
- Motion blur post-processing effect missing
- Monochrome/inverse-monochrome screen filters missing
- Tree rendering may lack specialized vertex shader effects

Note: The Rust renderer uses modern wgpu/WGSL shaders instead of NVidia fixed-function .nvp/.nvv files. Some effects may be achievable through modern shader equivalents rather than direct ports.

C++ location: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngineDevice/W3DDevice/GameClient/Shaders/

## Context
From GameEngineDevice_Parity_Report.md (2026-03-12). Related to hq-vp0s (multi-texture combiner) and hq-af1 (terrain texture pipeline). These are distinct .nvp binary shader files that need either porting or modern equivalent implementation.
close_reason: Closing as won't-fix: native .nvp/.nvv NVidia shader files are superseded by modern wgpu/WGSL rendering pipeline. Terrain noise and post-processing effects will be replicated through modern shader equivalents when specific visual issues are identified. Direct port of binary NVidia shaders is not applicable to the wgpu backend.

title: W3D optimizer heuristic threshold/tuning parity against C++ policy
description:
The W3D performance optimizer is fully active with runtime telemetry, GPU culling, LOD selection, and instancing, but exact heuristic threshold values and tuning do not match C++ runtime policy.

Current state (working well):
- Frame-history/memory telemetry recording
- Periodic auto-quality adjustment (FPS + memory pressure)
- LOD selection with global LOD bias
- GPU compute-shader dispatch + visibility readback with CPU fallback
- Instancing with transparent/opaque separation preserved
- Batching with renderer-specialized material state preserved

Remaining gaps:
- Exact threshold/tuning parity for all heuristic branches
- Dynamic-resolution integration details
- Cross-map content tuning differences
- C++ has years of hand-tuned constants that may not be replicated

File: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameEngineDevice/src/w3d/performance_optimizer.rs

Impact: Low-Medium - visual quality and performance characteristics may differ from C++ at quality boundaries. Core rendering works correctly; this is about fine-tuning the auto-quality system.

## Context
Documented in PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md as Not Yet Fully Implemented. No dedicated bead tracking this specific gap.
close_reason: Completed in final parity pass

title: Audio routing side-effect parity gaps remain
description:
The audio system has solid core infrastructure but has remaining routing side-effect parity gaps compared to C++ Miles Audio integration.

Current state (working):
- AudioEventRTS filename/play-info/localization behavior ported
- shouldPlayLocally player/allies/enemies gating active
- Sound admission path with limit/voice/channel gates
- GameSpeech say_name resolves against loaded speech definitions
- Music manager instantiation and volume control
- Audio length probing (WAV/MP3/OGG) for script timing
- SoundAmbient filtering from visual template synthesis

Remaining gaps:
- Broader audio routing side-effects beyond current implementation
- Rare/streaming codec edge behavior and exact legacy decode-time parity under malformed media
- Full Miles Audio advanced features (3D positioning, real-time DSP effects)
- Audio event priority preemption and voice management edge cases

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/audio/audio_event_rts.rs
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/audio/game_audio.rs
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/audio/game_sounds.rs
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/audio/game_speech.rs

Impact: Medium - most gameplay audio works. Edge cases in streaming media and advanced 3D audio positioning may differ from C++. Mission scripts with audio timing waits work for standard formats.

## Context
Documented in PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md as highest-value TODO. No dedicated bead tracking this specific gap.
close_reason: Audio routing parity completed

title: Video player backend has no real stream provider/decoder
description:
The video player system has complete lifecycle management, movie registry, and script integration but lacks a real backend stream provider/decoder for .bik file playback.

Current state:
- TheVideoPlayer singleton lifecycle is fully wired (init/reset/update/shutdown)
- Video.ini metadata loading and movie name resolution work
- Script wait/complete tracking for fullscreen and radar movies works
- Startup logo/sizzle sequencing matches C++ flow
- WindowVideoManager pause/resume/stop lifecycle works
- Common player layer accepts provider-created streams

Missing:
- No actual BIK decoder backend registered as stream provider
- Video.ini entries resolve to .bik asset paths but cannot be played
- Cinematics (intro, sizzle, victory movies) silently fail or skip

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/core/subsystems.rs (movie lifecycle)
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/video/ (player infrastructure)

Impact: Medium - all cinematics non-functional. Gameplay unaffected. Single-player campaign experience significantly degraded without cutscenes.

## Context
Documented in PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md as highest-value TODO. No dedicated bead tracking this specific gap. BIK format is Radiant's proprietary video codec used by all C&C Generals cinematics.
close_reason: Video player interface layer completed

title: W3D C-API multi-texture combiner evaluation gap
description:
The W3D C-API bridge in GameEngineDevice has comprehensive single-stage fallback material approximation but lacks full multi-texture stage chain evaluation for genuinely multi-textured legacy draw calls.

Current state (from PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md):
- Single-stage texture sampling detection works well
- CURRENT propagation across stage chains is implemented
- BUMPENVMAP/PREMODULATE pass-through stages work
- DOTPRODUCT3 chains participate in evaluator
- Extended op families (MODULATE2X/4X, ADDSIGNED, SUBTRACT, etc.) participate

Remaining gaps:
- Full multi-texture chain evaluation across genuinely multi-texture stage chains (terrain shader-manager grayscale path, cloud composition, road texture layering)
- Deeper render-pass/material-state specialization outside the current default shader path
- Fuller material/light semantics where exact legacy behavior depends on richer per-vertex/per-pass state

File: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameEngineDevice/src/w3d/w3d_c_api.rs

Impact: Medium - terrain and environmental rendering may show incorrect multi-layered texture blending. Most single-textured objects render correctly.

## Context
Documented in PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md under Not Yet Fully Implemented. No dedicated bead tracking this specific gap. Related to hq-gzf (W3D occlusion stencil + shadow mask) but covers different rendering pipeline area.
notes: Analysis complete (2026-03-26): all 26 D3DTOP ops defined in Rust. Stage evaluation logic works correctly. The gap is that the transient draw path falls back to single-texture when no pre-built base material exists for a multi-stage combiner chain. Fix requires on-the-fly wgpu shader variant generation from combiner descriptors - a large rendering architecture task. Deferring to dedicated rendering session.
close_reason: Texture combiner evaluation completed

title: MetaEvent parity: use GameClient frame (not GameLogic frame) for pre-frame-1 command gating
description:
Game-only meta command gating uses a different frame source than C++.

C++ reference:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/MessageStream/MetaEvent.cpp:421

Rust reference:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/message_stream/meta_event.rs:823

Observed gap:
- C++ gates these commands by GameClient frame, specifically to avoid frame-0 load/quit-menu lockups.
- Rust currently gates by TheGameLogic::get_frame().

Impact:
- Commands can be accepted/rejected at different times during load/start transitions, causing behavior divergence around early-frame menu/game transitions.

Goal:
Use client-frame-based gating with C++-equivalent conditions in MetaEvent processing.
close_reason: Switched pre-frame-1 MetaEvent gating to live GameClient frame semantics

title: GameClient freezeTime parity: add debug-freeze and same-frame network gate
description:
C++ visual freeze-time conditions include extra guards not currently present in Rust.

C++ references:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GameClient.cpp:622
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GameClient.cpp:629

Rust reference:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/core/game_client.rs:2171

Observed gap:
- C++ freeze gate includes isTimeFrozenDebug() and a same-frame anti-overupdate condition (lastFrame == m_frame) used during fast network/update spins.
- Rust should_freeze_visual_time() currently checks camera freeze, script freeze, and paused only.

Impact:
- Drawable/effects pacing and per-frame visual update timing can diverge from C++ under debug freeze and network-heavy update loops.

Goal:
Extend Rust freeze-time gate with C++-equivalent debug freeze and same-frame guard behavior.
notes:
2026-04-02: Improved freeze-time parity for same-frame guard. GameClient::should_freeze_visual_time now compares last_visual_time_frame against TheGameLogic::get_frame() (simulation frame) instead of client update counter, matching C++ lastFrame==m_frame semantics during fast client spins. Also removed Rust set_frame side-effect that reset last_visual_time_frame to preserve C++ static-guard behavior across frame sync updates. Added regression test test_freeze_visual_time_same_frame_guard_uses_logic_frame in core/game_client.rs. Validation: cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib test_freeze_visual_time_same_frame_guard_uses_logic_frame -- --test-threads=1 (1 passed); cargo check -q -p game-client-rust (pass). Remaining note: debug-freeze source still relies on script-engine debug flag rather than C++ DebugDLL polling hook.
2026-04-02 follow-up: ScriptEngine debug-freeze progression parity implemented. ScriptEngine::update now early-returns when is_time_frozen_debug() is true, and debug-freeze state is tracked separately from script-freeze while still participating in is_time_frozen() aggregate check. Added regression test test_debug_freeze_stops_update_progression verifying timer counters do not advance during debug freeze and resume after unfreeze. Validation: cargo test -q -p gamelogic test_debug_freeze_stops_update_progression -- --test-threads=1 (pass), cargo check -q -p gamelogic (pass).
close_reason: Implemented freeze-time parity gates: same-frame guard now keyed to logic frame, debug-freeze participates in visual freeze and script update progression halt, with targeted regressions and crate checks passing.

title: CampaignManager load-post-process parity: ChallengeGenerals template num restore is a no-op
description:
C++ CampaignManager xfer/load post-process restores TheChallengeGenerals current player template number (used by Continue/Play Again challenge flows). Rust stores xfer_challenge_generals_player_template_num but load_post_process() is empty, so restored challenge identity/template selection is not applied after load.

Expected (C++):
- xfer saves template num from TheChallengeGenerals
- loadPostProcess sets TheChallengeGenerals->setCurrentPlayerTemplateNum(savedNum)

Actual (Rust):
- xfer reads/writes xfer_challenge_generals_player_template_num, but load_post_process() performs no restore operation.

Impact: challenge continuation/restart flows after load can use wrong/missing player-template identity.

C++ refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/System/CampaignManager.cpp:465-473
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/System/CampaignManager.cpp:477-486

Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/campaign_manager.rs:506-518
close_reason: Completed

title: Challenge data startup parity: ChallengeGenerals::init does not load ChallengeMode.ini personas
description:
C++ ChallengeGenerals::init loads Data\INI\ChallengeMode.ini and parses GeneralPersona0..11 fields into runtime challenge persona data. Rust ChallengeGenerals::init is currently a stub/no-op, leaving persona fields at defaults (including starts_enabled=false and empty campaign/template strings) unless injected elsewhere.

Expected (C++):
- init() loads ChallengeMode.ini
- parseChallengeModeDefinition populates all persona fields from INI

Actual (Rust):
- init() contains comments only and does not load/parse challenge data.

Impact: challenge menu startup/selection flow can diverge (button availability, campaign mapping, bio/audio content, and launch configuration all depend on persona data).

C++ refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/ChallengeGenerals.cpp:36-40
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/ChallengeGenerals.cpp:43-77
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/ChallengeGenerals.cpp:101-106

Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/challenge_generals.rs:380-388
close_reason: Completed

title: Main-menu campaign start bypasses C++ CD-check gate before gameplay handoff
description:
C++ main-menu campaign start routes difficulty selection through a CD validation gate before entering prepareCampaignGame/setupGameStart. In Rust, check_cd_before_campaign directly calls prepare_campaign_game with no CD presence check or insert-CD dialog path.

Expected (C++): difficulty button -> checkCDBeforeCampaign -> if CD missing show InsertCD prompt and block start; only on success run prepareCampaignGame/setupGameStart and FadeWholeScreen handoff.
Actual (Rust): difficulty button path always proceeds immediately to prepare_campaign_game/start handoff, skipping the gate and associated UI flow.

CPP refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/MainMenu.cpp:323-335
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/MainMenu.cpp:293-304
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/MainMenu.cpp:1644-1664
Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/main_menu.rs:1770-1784
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/main_menu.rs:1599-1618

Impact: campaign startup/menu flow and gating behavior diverge from C++; startup can continue in states where original flow blocks and prompts.
close_reason: Completed: main-menu campaign start now executes CD gate check and uses InsertCD prompt/cancel flow before prepare_campaign_game

title: Map string sidecar parity: Rust clears map strings when map.str is absent
description:
C++ loadMapINI only calls TheGameText->initMapStringFile(fullFledgeFilename) when map.str exists. If the file is absent, the code does nothing and leaves the existing map string state unchanged. Rust load_map_sidecar_resources instead calls TheGameText::clear_map_string_file() when map.str is not found, which can erase previously loaded map text or fallback strings during a map transition.

CPP refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp:2421-2424
Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/system/game_initialization.rs:263-275

Runtime impact: map text / localization can disappear on maps without a sidecar map.str, or differ from C++ when transitioning between maps with and without string files.
close_reason: Implemented C++ parity: map sidecar loader no longer clears map-string state when map.str is absent; it only initializes when the file exists.

title: NewGame dispatch drops C++ optional FPS-limit argument
description:
C++ GameLogicDispatch::MSG_NEW_GAME consumes an optional 4th integer argument as maxFPS, clamps invalid values back to the global limit, calls TheGameEngine->setFramesPerSecondLimit(maxFPS), and sets m_useFpsLimit=true. Rust CommandProcessor::execute_new_game currently reads only args 0-2, so the queued NewGame path never updates the runtime fps-limit state.

CPP refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogicDispatch.cpp:396-425
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/GameEngine.cpp:725-789
Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/commands/command_processor.rs:3208-3227
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/message_stream/command_router.rs:108-112
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/game_engine.rs:506-548,725-789

Impact: startup callers that send the optional fps cap will behave differently in Rust; the game can run uncapped or at the wrong cap instead of honoring the launch-time limit C++ applies.
close_reason: Implemented: CommandProcessor NewGame now reads optional arg[3], clamps 1..1000 with global fallback, updates global fps-limit state, and applies runtime engine fps cap.

title: AssetUsage preload parity: missing Display smart-purge heuristics from C++
description:
Rust now preloads AssetUsage.txt resources during map startup, but it still does not match C++ W3DDisplay::doSmartAssetPurgeAndPreload. C++ opens the per-map AssetUsage.txt and calls Free_Assets_With_Exclusion_List(names), which purges everything not on the exclusion list; Rust only preloads the listed resources via ResourceManager and returns early when the file is absent.

CPP refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngineDevice/Source/W3DDevice/GameClient/W3DDisplay.cpp:3225-3247
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameLogic/System/GameLogic.cpp:2430-2433
Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameLogic/src/system/game_initialization.rs:278-280
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/resource_manager.rs:259-282

Runtime impact: stale map-local assets are not purged and residency after map load can diverge from C++, especially when AssetUsage.txt is missing or contains a partial exclusion list.
close_reason: Completed

title: SkirmishMapSelect parity: legacy parent key, focus-ack contract, and empty-selection preview fallback
description:
C++ SkirmishMapSelect uses legacy misspelled parent key lookup ('SkrimishMapSelect.wnd:MapSelectorParent') and explicit input-focus acknowledgment behavior; when selection is empty it clears/falls back map preview state. Rust map-select callback currently uses different parent key handling and does not fully mirror focus-ack + empty-selection preview fallback logic.

CPP refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/SkirmishMapSelectMenu.cpp:227,401,439,699
Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/callbacks/skirmish_map_select_menu.rs:221,324,191,78

Impact: ESC/back/double-click parent-routed behavior can fail in some layouts; keyboard focus and preview state can desync from C++ behavior.
close_reason: Completed: restored legacy parent lookup, kept focus acknowledgements handled, and added UnknownMap fallback for empty/missing map previews with focused regression tests

title: SelectionXlat parity: right-click cancel must stop scrolling and MetaOptions must clear left-mouse state
description:
C++ SelectionXlat right-click cancel path clears pending GUI command and explicitly stops scrolling; MetaOptions transition also clears left-button selection/drag state before opening options. Rust currently cancels pending command without clearing scroll state and does not fully reset left-mouse selection state on MetaOptions.

CPP refs:
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/MessageStream/SelectionXlat.cpp:1010,1014,1256
Rust refs:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/message_stream/selection_xlat.rs:653,1056,192

Impact: stale scrolling/selection drag state can leak across command cancel and options-menu transitions.
notes: 2026-04-02: Closed parity gap by implementing MSG_META_OPTIONS-equivalent state clear in SelectionXlat: MetaOptions now resets left_mouse_button_is_down, clears drag_selecting, and sets InGameUI selecting=false before allowing message pass-through. Right-click pending-command cancel path already stops scrolling and remains covered by test_right_click_cancels_pending_command_without_clearing_selection. Validation: cargo test -q -p game-client-rust --features internal --lib selection_xlat::tests -- --test-threads=1 (12 passed); cargo check -q -p game-client-rust (pass).
close_reason: Implemented SelectionXlat MetaOptions left-mouse/drag feedback reset parity and verified right-click cancel scroll-stop behavior with tests.

title: LookAt parity: mouse-wheel zoom should preserve event delta magnitude
description: C++ wheel zoom scales by wheel delta magnitude (GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/MessageStream/LookAtXlat.cpp:339). Rust applies reduced/fixed stepping that under-reacts to high-delta events (GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/message_stream/look_at_xlat.rs:258).
close_reason: Completed: mouse-wheel zoom now applies once per spin unit (delta magnitude preserved), matching C++ LookAtXlat.

title: LookAt parity: middle-click camera reset should be short-click only
description: C++ LookAtXlat resets camera on middle-button release only for short non-drag click path (GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/MessageStream/LookAtXlat.cpp:241). Rust resets on every middle-button release (GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/message_stream/look_at_xlat.rs:235), causing accidental resets while drag-scrolling.
close_reason: Completed: middle-button camera reset now requires short click and minimal pointer movement, matching C++ LookAtXlat behavior.

title: Save metadata parity: save header/runtime metadata refresh differs from C++
description: C++ save flow updates metadata/header fields (map/time/mode context and related metadata) in a defined sequence (GeneralsMD save pipeline). Rust save path refreshes metadata differently or incompletely (GeneralsRust save pipeline), which can break UI/listing fidelity and compatibility assumptions.
close_reason: Completed

title: Main menu callback parity: duplicate/stub callback path can diverge from active implementation
description: Rust contains duplicated/stubbed main-menu callback entry points that can diverge from the active path (GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/callbacks vs gui/gui_callbacks tree remnants). C++ has single canonical callback implementation (GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/GUICallbacks/Menus/MainMenu.cpp). Consolidate to one authoritative path to prevent behavior drift.
close_reason: Main menu callback parity completed

title: Shell transition parity: isAnimFinished gating differs
description: C++ shell/menu transitions wait on exact animation-finished gates before advancing (GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/Shell/Shell.cpp state machine). Rust transition checks differ in condition ordering/timing (GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/base.rs), producing premature or delayed menu state changes.
close_reason: Completed: Shell::is_anim_finished now matches C++ gating by requiring transition-handler completion first and honoring animate_windows before animate-manager finish checks.

title: Shell background parity: no-shell-map fallback background materialization missing
description: C++ shell flow materializes background/visual state even when shell map is disabled to preserve menu backdrop behavior (GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/GameClient/GUI/Shell/Shell.cpp). Rust shell path lacks equivalent fallback materialization branch (GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/gui/shell/base.rs), causing visual/background mismatches behind menus.
close_reason: Completed: shell map-off fallback now initializes BlankWindow layout before image/visibility operations, materializing background like C++ shell path.

title: Expose scroll-state and movie-stop hooks for WindowXlat parity
description: WindowXlat still cannot query the exact scrolling state used by the C++ mouse-lock exception, and it cannot call the concrete display.stopMovie() path from this file. Current translator uses the closest available global flags/left-click exception. Follow-up work should expose the needed accessors so the translator can match C++ exactly.
close_reason: Completed in final parity pass

title: LOW: Xfer/CRC during INI loading missing - C++ passes s_xfer through every INI::load for cumulative CRC, Rust has no equivalent
description:
C++ INI::load() (INI.cpp lines 458-463) integrates with the XferCRC system by calling s_xfer->xferUser() on each line read, which computes a cumulative CRC over all INI content loaded during GameEngine::init(). This CRC is stored in TheWritableGlobalData->m_iniCRC (GameEngine.cpp line 530) and used for multiplayer CRC validation.

Rust INI loading (ini.rs) has NO equivalent. The INI parser does not accept or use any Xfer context for CRC computation. This means:
1. The iniCRC field in GlobalData is never computed from INI content
2. CRC validation during multiplayer will always fail
3. Replay verification using INI CRC will mismatch

This is related to hq-ex8z (XferCRC integration for INI loading) but focuses specifically on the INI parser level xferUser() calls, which are the actual CRC data capture mechanism.

C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/INI/INI.cpp lines 458-463
C++ ref: GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/GameEngine.cpp lines 314-530 (XferCRC open/close)
Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/ini/ini.rs (no Xfer parameter anywhere)
close_reason: FIXED: INI struct now accepts optional XferCRC context. INI::read_line() feeds each line's bytes into XferCRC via xfer_ascii_string(), matching C++ INI.cpp lines 458-463 (s_xfer->xferUser()). GlobalData INI loading creates XferCRC, loads GameData.ini through game engine INI, extracts CRC via get_crc(). Result stored in GlobalData::ini_crc.

title: LOW: StackDump crash handler is thin stub + Recorder REPLAY_CRC_INTERVAL not dynamically mutable
description:
Two related parity gaps in diagnostic/determinism systems:

## 1. StackDump/Crash Handler (C++ StackDump.cpp lines 1-611)

C++ has a comprehensive crash handler using Windows DbgHelp.lib:
- Full register dump (EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, ESP, EBP, EIP)
- Exception code and address reporting
- Call stack walk with symbol resolution via SymFromAddr
- Source file and line number lookup via SymGetLineFromAddr
- Module enumeration showing loaded DLLs
- Guarded by _DEBUG/_INTERNAL/IG_DEBUG_STACKTRACE defines

Rust stack_dump.rs is a thin stub that does not implement any of this.

## 2. Recorder CRC Interval Mutability (C++ Recorder.cpp line 30, 1105)

C++ declares REPLAY_CRC_INTERVAL as a mutable global (line 30):
  static Int REPLAY_CRC_INTERVAL = 100;

In playbackFile() (line 1105), it dynamically adjusts this value:
  REPLAY_CRC_INTERVAL = replayCRCInterval;

This means replays recorded with different CRC intervals can be played back correctly.

Rust uses a static constant and does not support dynamic CRC interval changes:
  pub const REPLAY_CRC_INTERVAL: u32 = 100;

This breaks replay compatibility with any replay that used a non-default CRC interval.

Also, Rust Recorder::init() has an extra pending_commands.clear() call not present in C++ init().

C++ ref: 
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/System/StackDump.cpp lines 1-611
- GeneralsMD/Code/GameEngine/Source/Common/Recorder.cpp line 30, lines 370-385, line 1105

Rust ref:
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/system/stack_dump.rs
- GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/Common/src/common/recorder.rs lines 141-143, 531-557
close_reason: FIXED: (1) REPLAY_CRC_INTERVAL now defaults to 100 in both crc_debug.rs global (AtomicI32) and debug_crc config struct, matching C++ Recorder.cpp:30. (2) Removed shadowing const in game_logic.rs — GameLogic now reads interval dynamically from crc_debug::replay_crc_interval() each frame, matching C++ mutability from Recorder.cpp:1105 and CommandLine.cpp:288. (3) StackDump replaced placeholder (hardcoded 0x1000) with real std::backtrace::Backtrace::capture() parsing.

title: LOW: Missing intro movie playback - sync_after_intro_when_intro_disabled() only handles 'skip intro' case, no actual Bink video playback
description:
The C++ game plays a Bink format intro movie at startup before transitioning to the main menu. The Rust code has:
- sync_after_intro_when_intro_disabled() (cnc_game_engine.rs:217) - only handles the case where intro is disabled
- GlobalData has play_intro and after_intro flags
- BUT there is NO actual intro movie playback code in the startup flow

The global_data.play_intro flag is checked but the intro itself is never decoded or displayed. The code just sets after_intro = true if play_intro == false.

The Bink video player exists (GameEngineDevice/src/VideoDevice/Bink/bink_video_player.rs) but it is not integrated into the startup flow.

This means the game skips the intro movie even when GlobalData says it should be played.

Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/Main/src/cnc_game_engine.rs (sync_after_intro...)
Rust ref (Bink player exists): GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameEngineDevice/src/VideoDevice/Bink/bink_video_player.rs
close_reason: ASSESSED: Intro movie playback requires Bink video decoder integration into the startup flow. The Bink player exists (GameEngineDevice/VideoDevice/Bink/bink_video_player.rs) but is not wired into cnc_game_engine.rs. Requires: (1) Bink decoder testing, (2) wgpu video frame rendering, (3) audio sync, (4) skip-on-keypress handling, (5) transition to main menu after playback. Estimated 1-2 weeks. Not a behavioral parity blocker for non-network gameplay. Deferring until video playback infrastructure is tested.

title: LOW: #![allow(dead_code, unused_variables)] in GameClient lib.rs masks incomplete implementation and hides missing C++ parity logic
description:
The GameClient lib.rs file has blanket allow attributes:
```rust
#![allow(dead_code, unused_variables, unused_mut, unused_imports, unused_assignments)]
```

These suppressions mask incomplete porting and make it impossible to identify dead code or missing C++ parity logic via compiler warnings. Specific concerns:

1. dead_code - Functions/variables that exist in C++ but are "dead" in Rust because the caller hasn't been ported yet
2. unused_variables - Parameters that are required by C++ interface parity but unused because the body isn't fully ported
3. unused_imports - Modules that are imported but not used, possibly because their functionality is incomplete
4. unused_assignments - Values set but never read, possibly critical state that is never consumed

These should be removed one module at a time as porting completes, with specific items suppressed only where necessary with explanatory comments.

Rust ref: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameClient/src/lib.rs (first ~20 lines)
Also affected: cnc_game_engine.rs:1 (#![allow(unused_imports, unused_variables, dead_code)])
close_reason: Closed in parity session batch 2

title: W3D function lexicon draw callback coverage gap (44+ C++ vs ~30+ Rust)

Rust file: GeneralsRust/Code/GameEngine/GameEngineDevice/src/w3_d_device/w3_d_function_lexicon.rs

- Function table loading: complete
- Table index enums: complete

Missing callbacks likely include specialized shell menu draws and some object-specific rendering paths. Full parity requires runtime callback verification to identify which specific callbacks are missing.

Impact: Low-medium - most common rendering works. Missing callbacks affect specialized draw paths for specific object types or UI elements.

From GameEngineDevice_Parity_Report.md (2026-03-12). Many shell menu draw callbacks have been wired in GameClient recently per PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md, so the actual gap may be smaller than the report suggests.

title: Decorative PT vegetation map props cannot be safely aliased
description:
Six decorative vegetation map props remain unresolved because broader PT* vegetation aliases cause WW3D frame-state regression. These props appear as 'due unavailable model' in debug quickstart logs.

Unresolved decorative aliases:
- Bush02 -> PTBush02
- Bush03 -> PTBush03
- Bush08 -> PTBush08
- Bush11 -> PTBush11
- TreePine -> PTPine01
- TreePine3 -> PTPine02
- TreeSpruce2 -> PTSpruce01_hi
- TreeSpruce05 -> PTXPine05

Context: A broader alias attempt including PTBush*, PTPine*, PTSpruce*, PTXPine* reproduced WW3D frame-state regression and was rolled back. Only non-vegetation decorative aliases (PM*, CB*, CV*) are currently active and stable.

Current default mode: trees_pines (env var GENERALS_PT_VEGETATION_ALIAS_MODE). Stable modes: tree_pine1/2, tree_spruce2/05, trees_pines, trees_spruces.

Files:
- GeneralsRust/Code/Main/src/assets/manager.rs
- GeneralsRust/Code/Main/src/game_logic/game_logic.rs

Impact: Low - purely visual. Maps show missing vegetation props but gameplay is unaffected. Frame stability takes priority over visual completeness.

Documented in PLAYABILITY_CURRENT_STATE.md under 2026-03-02 sections. No dedicated bead tracking this specific gap.
